entry.title: Joule A2A: Secure Code Based Agents with IAS
entry.content:
In the previous blog post, we explored how to integrate a Pro-Code Agent into the Joule ecosystem. While the initial setup functioned correctly, it did not include proper authentication, leaving the agent accessible to anyone on the internet.

In this post, we address that gap by implementing secure access controls. Specifically, we will demonstrate how to protect your agent using SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication Service (IAS) in a Python-based setup.

Scenario

Our target is to use SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication Service (short: IAS) to take care of the authentication. We will implement the authentication check on the Python side using middleware that validates JWT tokens from IAS.

Important distinction: In this blog, we focus on protecting the agent from unauthorized access using the OAuth2 Client Credentials flow. This is app-to-app authentication - we verify that the calling application (e.g., Joule) has valid credentials to access our agent. The token identifies the application, not the end user.

If your agent needs to call backend systems on behalf of the actual user (e.g., accessing SAP S/4HANA with the user's permissions), you'll need Principal Propagation - forwarding the user's identity through the call chain. That's a different pattern which I'll cover in a follow-up blog post.

By the end of this blog, you will have a secure A2A agent that validates JWT tokens and IAS configured with the necessary applications and API scopes.


Prerequisites

For the IAS setup basics, please refer to my previous blog post: Using IAS to Secure Python APIs on Cloud Foundry. That post covers:

  • Creating an application in IAS
  • Configuring Provided APIs (scopes like api_read_access)
  • Creating client credentials with API permissions

So please follow the chapter "Creating an Application in IAS" closely and then get started here.

For this scenario, you will need:

  1. IAS Tenant: Access to an SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant with an application configured
  2. Client Credentials: Client ID and Client Secret with api_read_access scope
  3. BTP Cloud Foundry: A Cloud Foundry space to deploy the agent
  4. SAP AI Core: For the agent's LLM capabilities (optional)

The overall setup of the A2A Integration is explained in my previous blog post regarding A2A. We are building upon that one here.


Part 1: Understanding JWT Token Verification

Before diving into the code, let's understand what we're actually verifying. When a client authenticates with IAS using the client credentials flow, IAS returns a JWT (JSON Web Token). This token is a Base64-encoded JSON object with three parts: header, payload, and signature.

Here's what a decoded IAS token payload looks like:

{
  "ias_apis": [
    "api_read_access",
    "api_write_access"
  ],
  "sub": "<your-client-id>",
  "aud": "<your-client-id>",
  "sap_id_type": "app",
  "azp": "<your-client-id>",
  "iss": "https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com",
  "exp": 1775566673,
  "iat": 1775563073,
  "jti": "0324db00-39ab-4dc5-888c-11fd4959aef5"
}

The key fields our middleware needs to verify are: iss (issuer) confirms the token came from our IAS tenant, aud (audience) confirms the token is meant for our application, exp (expiration) ensures the token hasn't expired, and ias_apis contains the scopes we configured in IAS - this is where we check for api_read_access.

The token is cryptographically signed by IAS. To verify this signature, our middleware fetches the public keys from IAS's JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) endpoint at /oauth2/certs. This ensures nobody can forge a valid token - only IAS can create tokens that pass verification.


Part 2: The IAS Authentication Middleware

Now let's look at the implementation. The middleware is intentionally minimal - about 50 lines of code:

"""IAS Authentication Middleware for A2A Server."""

import os

import jwt
import requests
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse

# Config - Load from environment variables
ISSUER = os.environ.get("IAS_ISSUER", "https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com")
JWKS_URL = f"{ISSUER}/oauth2/certs"
AUDIENCE = os.environ.get("IAS_AUDIENCE", "your-client-id-here")


class IASAuthMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    """Validates JWT tokens from SAP IAS."""

    def get_public_key(self, token: str):
        """Fetch JWKS and find the matching public key."""
        kid = jwt.get_unverified_header(token)["kid"]
        jwks = requests.get(JWKS_URL).json()

        for key in jwks["keys"]:
            if key["kid"] == kid:
                return jwt.algorithms.RSAAlgorithm.from_jwk(key)

        raise Exception("No matching key found")

    def verify_token(self, token: str):
        """Validate JWT token and return payload."""
        public_key = self.get_public_key(token)
        payload = jwt.decode(
            token, public_key, algorithms=["RS256"],
            audience=AUDIENCE, issuer=ISSUER
        )

        if "api_read_access" not in payload.get("ias_apis", []):
            raise Exception("Missing required ias_apis scope")

        return payload

    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        # Allow public discovery endpoints
        if request.url.path.startswith("/.well-known/"):
            return await call_next(request)

        auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")

        if not auth_header or not auth_header.startswith("Bearer "):
            return JSONResponse(status_code=401, content={"detail": "Missing token"})

        token = auth_header.split(" ")[1]

        try:
            payload = self.verify_token(token)
            request.state.user = payload
        except Exception as e:
            return JSONResponse(status_code=401, content={"detail": str(e)})

        return await call_next(request)

The get_public_key method fetches the JWKS from IAS and finds the key matching the token's kid (key ID) header. The verify_token method uses PyJWT to decode and verify the token in one step - it checks the signature, issuer, audience, and expiration automatically. We then add our custom check for the api_read_access scope in the ias_apis claim. The dispatch method is called for every request and either lets it through (for /.well-known/ paths) or validates the Bearer token.

Note: Some endpoint are meant to be public - /.well-known/ contains the agent cards and is used for the discovery. Thus we want to exclude that from the auth checks.


Part 3: Integrating with the A2A Server

Adding the middleware to your A2A server is straightforward - just one line:

from a2a.server.apps import A2AStarletteApplication
from middleware.ias_auth import IASAuthMiddleware

# ... setup agent_card and request_handler ...

server = A2AStarletteApplication(
    agent_card=agent_card, http_handler=request_handler
)

# Build app and add IAS auth middleware
app = server.build()
app.add_middleware(IASAuthMiddleware)


Deployment Configuration

Set the environment variables in your manifest.yaml:

applications:
- name: currency-agent
  memory: 512M
  buildpack: python_buildpack
  command: uvicorn app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port ${PORT}
  env:
    IAS_ISSUER: "https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com"
    IAS_AUDIENCE: "your-client-id"

The IAS_ISSUER is relating to your IAS tenant URL. The IAS_AUDIENCE to the respective client. In our setup this matches to the client_id of the application we maintained in IAS.

Deploy with:

cd app
cf push


Part 4: Testing the Authentication

Let's verify that our authentication is working correctly.


Step 1: Test Public Endpoint (No Auth Required)

The agent card is accessible without authentication for A2A discovery:

curl -s "https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/.well-known/agent.json" | jq '.'

Response:

{
  "name": "Currency Agent",
  "description": "Helps with exchange rates for currencies",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "protocolVersion": "0.3.0",
  "capabilities": {
    "pushNotifications": true,
    "streaming": true
  },
  "skills": [
    {
      "id": "convert_currency",
      "name": "Currency Exchange Rates Tool",
      "description": "Helps with exchange values between various currencies"
    }
  ]
}


Step 2: Test Without Token (Should Fail)

We use the JSON-RPC syntax for doing a HTTP request - sending a basic message construct.

curl -s "https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "message/send", "params": {}, "id": "1"}'

Response:

As expected - we get the HTTP 401 - Missing Token Error we implemented.

{
  "detail": "Missing token"
}


Step 3: Get Token and Call Agent

Using our IAS Tenant URL to retrieve a Token giving our client credentials. Then doing an actual message send via the A2A syntax. This time we actually include some text message.

# Get token from IAS
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST "https://<tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  -d "client_id=<client-id>" \
  -d "client_secret=<client-secret>" | jq -r '.access_token')

# Call the agent
curl -s "https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "method": "message/send",
    "params": {
      "message": {
        "role": "user",
        "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "What is 100 USD in EUR?"}],
        "messageId": "msg-123"
      }
    },
    "id": "1"
  }' | jq '.'

Response:

{
  "id": "1",
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "result": {
    "artifacts": [
      {
        "artifactId": "ad8b315f-dd87-45ca-9f5e-584439102732",
        "name": "conversion_result",
        "parts": [
          {
            "kind": "text",
            "text": "100 USD is approximately 86.77 EUR based on the current exchange rate."
          }
        ]
      }
    ],
    "contextId": "095d62bd-dfa1-4b77-991b-392fe7038ecd",
    "id": "639c1a4b-d486-45df-b4be-4b614e0c441d",
    "kind": "task",
    "status": {
      "state": "completed",
      "timestamp": "2026-04-07T11:44:14.740338+00:00"
    }
  }
}

And as a result we actually get the converted currency - presenting the auth header!

Part 5: Connecting to Joule

Now that your agent is secured with IAS authentication, you need to configure Joule to communicate with it via a BTP Destination. Last time we configured the Destination without any authentication. This time we opt for OAuth2ClientCredentials.

Create the BTP Destination

In your BTP Cockpit, create a destination for your secured agent:

1. Navigate to Connectivity > Destinations
2. Create a new destination with these settings:
- Name: CURRENCY_AGENT
- Type: HTTP
- URL: https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com
- Proxy Type: Internet
- Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
- Client ID: <Your IAS Client ID>
- Client Secret: <Your IAS Client Secret>
- Token Service URL: https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token

Note: Since this blog builds upon the last one - ensure that you have already created a cabability in Joule that points a agent-request towards this destination. Once you created / updated this destination - make sure to update the Joule Capability. Because Joule is apparently caching the Destination content for some time. You can use joule delete and joule deploy to do so!

Test from Joule

Once the destination is configured, you can interact with the secured agent through Joule:

 


Conclusion

With just ~50 lines of middleware code, we've secured our A2A agent with IAS authentication. The middleware validates JWT tokens from IAS, checks the required scopes, and keeps the agent discovery endpoints public for A2A protocol compliance. The full code is available on GitHub.

I hope you found this insightful. Feel free to leave questions in the comments!

entry.title: Custom Joule Agent for SAP Ariba SLP - Building an AI Agent for Supplier Registration Approval
entry.content:
The Premise

I'll be honest — I've built this exact workflow before.

In previous posts, I put together a supplier registration approval automation using SAP Build Process Automation. It worked, but it took me a significant amount of time to complete: process flows, variables, connections, data mappings, error handling — each piece designed and wired manually. You can check out those articles Extending SLP functionalities with SAP BTP and Artificial Intelligence  and SAP Ariba SLP - Optical character recognition and Extraction for Supplier Registration Process .

This time, I completed a fully functional proof of concept in less than 7 days.

Same business problem. Same outcome. A fraction of the effort.

Going in, I had all the questions a developer instinctively asks: Who manages the data and how? Where do variables live? How are connections handled? What happens when something fails? These are the questions that typically consume the first week of any integration project before a single line of logic is written.

With Joule Studio, most of those questions answered themselves. The agent managed context between steps, retained the values I needed across the workflow, and handled the SAP Ariba skill connections natively — often without me explicitly instructing it to. The infrastructure just disappeared.

What remained was the part that actually matters: defining the business logic, the scoring model, the decision rules, and the communication to the supplier. The work that requires domain knowledge, not plumbing.

The Real Cost of Manual Supplier Review

Every supplier onboarding in SAP Ariba SLP follows the same predictable path. The supplier fills out a registration questionnaire. An approval task lands in someone's queue. That someone opens it, reads through the answers, checks the numbers, looks for the documents, and makes a call: approve, send it back, or reject.

Sounds simple. It really doesn't feel that way at month-end when the queue has 40 tasks in it.

Here's what always got me: most of what a reviewer is doing in that queue isn't actually a judgment call. They're reading a free-text answer asking themselves — does this BCP response mention an RPO? Does this AML section describe a whistleblowing channel? They're checking if a growth rate in one field matches a balance change in another. They're confirming a document is attached. That's not analysis. That's a checklist. A very slow, very manual, very inconsistent checklist being run by people whose time is worth a lot more than that.

And the problems that came from it were real:

  • Reviews varied wildly depending on who did them — the same questionnaire could sail through with one reviewer and generate three rounds of back-and-forth with another, often over a misspelled word or an answer that was technically correct but phrased oddly.
  • Tasks just sat there — not because nobody cared, but because reviewers had other priorities and supplier registration wasn't always the urgent thing. Suppliers would wait days on submissions that should have been a five-minute review.
  • Nothing was documented — approvals went through with no written rationale. The moment someone asked "why was this supplier approved?" the answer was essentially "because someone clicked approve."
  • Language became a routing problem — suppliers in Argentina or Brazil submitting in Spanish or Portuguese meant we couldn't just assign the task to anyone. It either waited for the right person or got reviewed by someone who couldn't properly read the answers.

None of these are hard problems. They're consistency problems. And the fix isn't hiring more reviewers — it's stopping the pattern-matching work from reaching a human in the first place.

That's where this started.

How it works?

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

How it looks?

 

How can I build my own?

Before anything else, you'll need to set up Joule Studio inside SAP Build. If you haven't done that yet, this guide walks you through it - How to get Started with Joule Studio 

Once you're in, you'll notice two new options in the Build lobby: Create a Joule Agent and Create a Joule Skill. Think of skills as the hands of the agent — they are the individual actions it can take. The agent is the brain that decides when and how to use them.

For this project, we built 4 custom skills, each one mapped to a specific SAP Ariba API call:

SkillWhat It Does
getPendingTasksRetrieves all active approval tasks from SAP Ariba SLP
getTaskDetailsFetches full task data including document ID, URL, and approval chain
getQuestionsAnswersPulls the supplier's questionnaire responses by document ID
postDecisionPosts the approval decision and comment back to the task in Ariba

These four skills are the only connection points between the agent and SAP Ariba. Everything else — the analysis, the scoring, the decision logic, the multilingual handling — lives entirely inside the agent configuration. No additional middleware, no custom code.

In Joule Studio, the foundation of every skill is the destination and the action endpoint. This is fundamentally different from how SAP Build Process Automation handles connectivity. In BPA, you could get away with loosely configured connections and patch things as you went. In Joule Studio, that doesn't work. Each skill needs a correctly defined destination in SAP BTP — the right authentication, the right base URL, the right headers — and each action within that skill needs a precisely mapped endpoint. Get either of those wrong and the skill fails silently, or worse, returns data you can't trust.

Check the SAP BTP Destinations section, it will give you the base for setting Destinations - End to End guide to setup Joule, SAP Build Process Automation, and SAP Build Work Zone on SAP BTP 

Let's make this concrete. Below is the actual configuration of getPendingTasks — the first skill the agent calls every time it runs. On the left you can see the destination binding pointing to the SAP Ariba environment. Below it, the action defines the specific endpoint the skill hits, the HTTP method, and the parameters it passes. This is the complete bridge between Joule Studio and Ariba. No code, no transformation layer — just a correctly configured destination and a precisely defined action

getPendingTasks Joule Skill

Pending Task Destination Response

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

What about the AI Agent?

Once your skills are ready, you create the agent. Joule Studio gives you three sections to configure. Each one has a distinct job — and understanding the boundary between them is what makes the difference between an agent that works reliably and one that kind of works most of the time.

 

Expertise — Who the agent is

This is where you define the agent's identity, role, and domain knowledge. Not what it does step by step, but what it knows going in. Think of it as the briefing you'd give a new analyst before their first day on the job.

In our case, we defined the agent as Joule, embedded in SAP Ariba SLP, with deep knowledge of supplier onboarding workflows, approval mechanics, questionnaire interpretation, and the SLP object model. We spelled out the domain concepts — tasks, workspaces, approval requests, registration projects — so the agent could interpret ambiguous API data through the right lens. When a financial number looked inconsistent, it needed procurement context to judge it correctly. This section provided that foundation.

Instructions — What the agent does

This is the behavioral core. A sequential set of rules that tells the agent exactly how to act, in what order, and under what conditions. Every step, every trigger, every output format lives here.

In our case, this section contained the full seven-step workflow: filtering tasks, building the results table, triggering the questionnaire analysis on a specific user command, running the three-part scoring analysis automatically, and posting the decision without waiting for confirmation. We also defined the comment templates for each decision type and the language rule for multilingual suppliers here. If the Expertise section is the briefing, Instructions is the playbook.

Additional Context — The rules the agent looks up

This is the reference layer. Static mappings, lookup tables, scoring rubrics, decision logic, and field definitions that the agent consults during execution. It doesn't drive behavior directly — Instructions does that — but it gives the agent the structured data it needs to make the right calls.

In our case, this section held everything that would be too verbose to embed in Instructions: the full decision mapping (Approve / Deny / DenyWithResubmit with priority rules), the answer lookup table mapping qualifiedNumber fields to their meaning, the complete scoring checklists for all four questions, the financial flag thresholds, the compliance flag definitions, and the language rule for comment output. Keeping this separate from Instructions made both sections easier to read, easier to update, and easier to debug when something didn't behave as expected.

TIP💡 A rule of thumb that saved us a lot of iteration: if it describes behavior, it belongs in Instructions. If it describes data or rules the behavior depends on, it belongs in Additional Context. Mixing the two is the most common reason agent configurations become hard to maintain.

Final Thoughts

I want to be honest about something before wrapping this up.

This agent doesn't replace human judgment. It was never meant to. There will always be submissions that land in a grey area — answers that technically check the boxes but still feel incomplete, numbers that pass the threshold but don't quite add up, situations that no rulebook covers cleanly. Those still need a person to look at them, for that we can add a "Human node" on Ariba SLP to make sure a human reviews AI decision.

What the agent does is make sure those are the only cases that reach a human. Everything else — the clean submissions, the straightforward approvals, the obvious denials — gets handled automatically, documented properly, and closed. In most onboarding queues, that's the majority of the work.

What genuinely surprised me wasn't the time I saved, though going from a much longer build down to seven days is hard to dismiss. It was how differently I had to think about the problem.

A few things I'd tell you to pay attention to before you start:

  • Do not rush the destinations. I know it looks like a simple setup step. It isn't. Get the BTP destination and the endpoint actions right before you touch anything else. Every problem I had early on traced back to this. HERE IS WHERE YOU DATA TYPE WILL BE DEFINED.
  • Put the real work into Additional Context. The scoring rubrics, the decision mapping, the language rules — that's where the agent gets smart. A generic configuration gives you a generic agent. The domain-specific rules are what make it actually useful.
  • Test with messy data, not just happy paths. Run a clean approval, a DenyWithResubmit with missing elements, a hard Deny, and at least one Spanish or Portuguese submission. If those four scenarios work correctly, you're in good shape.
  • Always filter on title, not description. The description field can come back empty from the API. I learned this the hard way so you don't have to.

Last but no least let me share some value insights of this PoC

entry.title: What's New in SAP BTP - Q1 2026 Innobytes
entry.content:
This first quarter has set the tone for 2026, bringing in new ideas and innovations all around Data, Joule and more. Let’s dive into some of the highlights Q1 has brought us.

 

 

AI & Data Foundation Highlight

Discovery and Data Agent in SAP HANA Cloud

The new Discovery and Data Agents are lowering learning barriers for developers and data users who just need answers. These new agents make it easier to explore, analyze, and act on data without needing deep knowledge of the underlying artifacts.

To see this demo in action, watch the video (starting at 0:11).

Learn more

  • Discovery & Data Agent on SAP Community
  • Register for the SAP HANA Cloud Data & Discovery Agents - Beta Program

 

Integration Highlight

Fresh Connections in SAP Integration Suite

New adapters for SAP Integration Suite on SAP BTP help transform your integration landscape, breaking down data silos, streamlining operations, and allow rapid adoption of new capabilities to innovate faster. 

The latest release offers:

  • The BambooHR adapter enables receiving and processing employee lifecycle data effectively.
  • The Microsoft Outlook Sender and Receiver adapter allows seamless integration for calendars, mail, attachments, and messaging directly into your mission-critical workflows. 

For Edge Integration Cella:

  • The File adapter, enabling secure file exchange with NFS systems
  • The OFTP2 adapter for compliant B2B connections to securely exchange messages from business partners. 

See the new integration content (starting at 2:14).

Learn more:

  • Integration Adapter
  • SAP Integration Suite on SAP Community

 

Agent & Application Development Highlights

API based Action Error Handling

In real-world automation, failures are inevitable. What matters is how the system handles them. This innovation brings precision and predictability to API error handling in SAP Build Process Automation on SAP BTP. When configuring an API-based Action step, enable error handling and define specific errors, handling authentication failures precisely. You can create generic categories, such as 4XX or 5XX, handling broader client or server issues.

The inability to define category overlap eliminates ambiguity, and deterministic runtime behavior is guaranteed. When an error occurs, precedence is strictly enforced:

  • Specific errors first
  • Generic category second
  • Undefined fallback last

At the Action level, you can configure retries that define the number of attempts and the delay between them. Afterwards, the error handling logic is executed. Every stage is fully logged in Monitoring, ensuring complete traceability and turning error handling from a black box into a controlled, observable workflow decision.

Watch the demo (starting at 3:35).

 

Conversational Work in SAP Build Work Zone 

There is now a powerful new way to work in SAP Build Work Zone. Save time by simply asking Joule what you need instead of clicking through multiple pages.

In the demo (starting at 5:33), a new project workspace for a cross-functional initiative is set up through a short conversation with Joule, enabling easy navigation, no technical steps, and only one screen. SAP Build Work Zone becomes more conversational, more intuitive, and more aligned with how people actually work.

Learn more:

  • Joule in SAP Build Work Zone

 

Core Platform Highlight

SAP Automation Pilot and Joule: Unlocking AI-Powered Ops Agents

The SAP Automation Pilot and Joule turn repeatable operational tasks into intelligent, agent-driven capabilities on SAP BTP.

By starting with a real operational process like a S/4HANA health check, use Automation Pilot’s low-code/no-code interface. Here, the necessary steps, inputs and validations can be collected and grouped into a Model Context Protocol (MCP) Server. The MCP Server exposes these automations as governed, reusable tools that can be safely consumed by AI agents without writing custom APIs.

Once published, connect it to Joule Studio to build a Joule Ops Agent that understands when and how to invoke these tools. The Ops Agent can now detect issues or fetch system status, triggering the right automation behind the scenes.

The DevOps or Admin team can simply ask the Ops Agent in Joule to ‘check my HANA Cloud and App instances health and remediate common issues’ by using the MCP Server to execute these steps securely, consistently, and audibly.

For a demo, watch the video (starting at 7:35).

Learn more:

  • Blog Post: From Automation to Agentic Ops
  • Blog Post: Meet your Ops Agents

 

Share your thoughts 

These videos are for YOU, and we would love to hear your impressions. How do you like the video? How can we improve upcoming videos? Please share your feedback by liking this post or leaving a comment below.

Thank you for your support and happy viewing, sharing, and enjoying!

You Might Also Be Interested In

  • SAP BTP Garage: monthly LIVE webinar with hands-on use case walkthroughs and demos.
  • Unlocking SAP BTP: monthly podcast covering all things SAP BTP.

entry.title: How SAP Joule is Transforming the Developer Experience
entry.content:
In today’s rapidly evolving tech landscape, being an ABAP developer is no longer just about writing code. To stay relevant and competitive, developers must embrace AI-powered tools that enhance productivity, improve code quality, and accelerate delivery.

One such innovation is SAP Joule, an AI assistant designed specifically for SAP developers. Integrated directly into ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse, Joule brings generative AI capabilities into your daily development workflow - without requiring you to switch between tools.

Let’s explore how Joule is transforming ABAP development.

What is SAP Joule for Developers?

SAP Joule is an AI-powered assistant that supports developers throughout the entire development lifecycle - from writing and understanding code to testing, optimization, and even migration.

Think of it as a smart coding partner that understands your context and helps you work faster and smarter.

 

Key features of Joule for Developers :

   1. Chat and Help feature    

Joule Chat allows you to interact using simple, natural language.

  • Ask questions about ABAP concepts
  • Get instant help from SAP documentation
  • Retrieve context-aware answers based on your work

You can also use commands like:

  • /help > for assistance
  • /explain > to understand code or objects

                    

   2. Code Understanding and Assistance 

Understanding legacy or complex code is often time-consuming. Joule simplifies this with its Explain feature.

It provides clear explanations for:

  • ABAP Classes
  • ABAP Programs
  • ATC findings
  • CDS artifacts

 Example;

/explain _view_name​

        Alternatively, Right-click on objects > Joule > Explain        

 

   3. Predictive Code Completion

Joule enhances your coding speed with AI-driven suggestions.

  • Auto-completes code intelligently
  • Works for classes, interfaces, and programs
  • Accept suggestions instantly using Tab

You can even write a requirement as a comment, and Joule will generate the code for you as ghost text.

       Settings 
Go to Window > Preferences > Source Code Editors > Code Completion, then review or update your settings. Make sure the "Trigger predictive code completion" option is enabled.

 

To display predictive suggestions in a dialog optimized for screen readers, go to Window > Preferences > ABAP Development > Accessibility > Predictive Code Completion.

You can manually trigger suggestions using Alt + J (Windows) or Option + J (Mac). Automatic triggering can also be turned on or off through the search dialog (Ctrl + 3).

 

 

To display proposals in a dialog box, enable the "Show Proposals in a dialog box" option.

 

To receive a suggested solution, write your requirement in plain commented text. The proposed solution will appear as ghost text. Press Tab to accept it.

 

4. Testing Support 

Writing unit tests becomes significantly easier with Joule.

  • Automatically generates ABAP Unit tests
  • Supports global and local classes
  • Generates test classes for CDS entities

This improves:

  • Test coverage
  • Code reliability
  • Developer productivity

To create an ABAP Unit test, right-click on the class and choose Joule > Start Chat for ABAP Unit. The Joule perspective will then present recommended next steps.

     
 
  
 

In Joule Chat, click "Add Tests to New Test Class".

                     
 
Joule will suggest ABAP Unit tests, giving you options to Apply, Insert, Copy, Refactor, or Generate Test.
 
 
 

  5. Development  

Joule can help you build applications faster by generating key components.

RAP Business Logic

  • Generates determinations and validations
  • Uses simple descriptions to create logic

 

Enter the requirement and specify what each method is expected to perform.

The proposal is generated based on your method description, and you can modify it manually if needed.

OData UI Services

  • Create complete applications from scratch
  • Automatically generates:
    • Service definitions
    • CDS views
    • Behavior definitions
    • Database tables

This significantly reduces manual effort in building applications.

Steps to create an OData UI application:

  1. Right-click on the package and select Generate ABAP Repository Objects.

     2. Choose OData UI Service from Scratch.          

         3. In the Configure Generator window, enter a description of your BO in Joule Chat. For example:           

        4.   Based on your prompt, Joule will suggest the BO entities and fields. If they match your requirements, click Accept. You can also modify them manually or with Joule’s assistance. Once satisfied, click Next.              

 

        5. Review the list of artifacts to be generated, then click Next. Select the Transport Request and click Finish.                    

 

Consume OData

The Consume capability in Joule lets you generate OData requests and corresponding ABAP code via the OData Client Proxy.
You need a service consumption model with a model class, created in ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse using the service metadata.

Steps:

  • Open the service consumption model of your OData service.
  • Start Joule chat:
    • Right-click an object > Joule > Consume, or
    • Open Joule chat and type /consume.

How It Works

  • Known OData request: Enter it directly (e.g., GET /Travel?$select=ID) and Joule will generate the corresponding ABAP code.
  • Need help creating a request: Describe your goal in plain language, and Joule will generate both the OData request and ABAP code.

You can adjust the generated request or code and try again.

 

  6. Migration  

Joule migration assistant helps you:

  • Understand ATC findings
  • Identify obsolete or incompatible code
  • Adapt to data model changes

This reduces risk and speeds up migration projects.

 

SAP Joule is more than just a development tool - it’s a game changer for ABAP developers. By integrating AI into everyday workflows, it helps developers write cleaner code, automate repetitive tasks, and quickly understand complex business objects.

entry.title: SAP DatabricksでAIアシスタントAPIを作ってみた
entry.content:
SAPデータを利用して気軽に質問に答えてくれるAI Agentを作りたい！Jouleから質問してSAPデータについて分析をしてほしい！と思ったことはありませんか？本ブログでは、SAP Databricksを利用したJoule用のAIアシスタントAPIの作成方法をご紹介します。

今回のブログを実行していただくことで、SAPデータを利用してDatabricksでAutoMLを利用した予測を作成し、その予測を利用したAIチャットが可能なアシスタントを簡単に作成することが可能です。外部のツールからもこのAIアシスタントを呼び出せるようになります！

本ブログは二部構成となっております。

1. SAP Databricksを利用した予測・AIアシスタント・APIエンドポイントの作成

2. SAP BTPでアプリケーションを作成・Joule Skillの作成

前提条件

  • インターネット接続環境
  • Webブラウザ（Chrome、Firefox、Safari推奨）
  • GitHubアカウント（リポジトリアクセス用）
  • SAP DatabricksもしくはDatabricksのアカウントへのアクセス

手順

※今回はハンズオン用のデータを利用します。SAP Databricksをご利用の方は、ご自身の環境のデータプロダクトを利用して同様のAIアシスタントAPIの作成が可能です。 

①教材リポジトリのクローン

  1. 左サイドバーの「Workspace」アイコンをクリック

  2. ツリー構造で「Users」→「あなたのメールアドレス」フォルダを展開

  3. あなたのフォルダを右クリックし、コンテキストメニューから「Create」を選択し、「Git folder」を選択

  4. Create画面で以下を設定：

    • Git repository URL:
      https://github.com/ureshiii-yu/dbx_handson
       
    • Git provider: 「GitHub」を選択
    • Git folder name: dbx_handson（自動入力）

  5. 「Gitフォルダを作成」ボタンをクリック

  6. クローン完了後、左サイドバーのWorkspaceツリーにdbx_handsonフォルダが表示されることを確認

②データ準備の実行

  1. Workspaceからdbx_handsonフォルダを展開

  2. 「0. データ準備」ノートブックをクリックして開く

  3. ノートブック画面右上の「Connect」ボタンをクリック

  4. コンピュートオプションから「Serverless」を選択し、「Connect」をクリック

  5. コンピュートの接続完了を待機（ステータスが緑色の「Connected」になるまで）

  6. ノートブック上部の「Run all」ボタンをクリック

  7. すべてのセルの実行完了まで待機（各セルの左側に緑のチェックマークが表示）

③データ作成の確認

  1. 左サイドバーの「Catalog」アイコンをクリック

  2. カタログブラウザで以下の構造が作成されていることを確認：

    • workspace → default → テーブル一覧
    • 作成されたテーブルが表示されていることを確認
       

④特徴量テーブルの作成

  1. Workspaceに戻り、「1. AI分析」ノートブックを開く

  2. ノートブック右上の「Connect」ボタンからServerlessコンピュートをアタッチ

  3. ノートブックの最初のセル（特徴量テーブル作成用）を実行

    • セル左上の「▶」ボタンをクリック、またはShift + Enter

  4. セル実行完了を確認（緑のチェックマーク表示）

  5. 2つ目のセルを実行し、テーブルにデータが入っているか確認する

⑤AutoMLの実行

  1. 左サイドバーの「Experiments」アイコンをクリック

  2. 「Forecast」ボタンをクリック

     

  3. AutoML設定画面で以下を入力：

    Data Configuration:

    • Training dataset: 手順6で作成した特徴量テーブルを選択 例: workspace.default.feature_sales_by_product
    • Time column: FISCAL_YEAR_MONTH
    • Forecast frequency: Monthly
    • Forecast horizon: 3 を入力
    • Prediction Target column: 売上データの対象列を選択 例:TOTAL_NETAMOUNT
    • Prediction data path: workspace.default（任意）
    • Prediction Table name: forecast_predictions_ureshino（任意）
    • Model registration Register to location: workspace.default（任意）
    • Model name: forecast_model_ureshino（任意）
       

    Advanced options を展開

    • Experiment name: forecast_experiment_ureshino（任意）
    • Time series identifier columns: REGION
    • Holiday region: Japan
    • Timeout: 15 minutes
    • そのほかの項目は自動入力のまま
       

  4. 「Start AutoML」ボタンをクリック

  5. AutoMLの実行開始を確認し、完了まで待機（約15分）
    Databricks のAutoMLでは、様々なモデルで予測を作成し実行結果を比較して確認することができます。

  6.  

  7. 自動的にPrediction Table name　ができているか確認する
     

⑥結果確認関数の作成

  1. 「1. AI分析」ノートブックに戻る

  2. 結果確認用の関数セルを実行（約5分）

    • 「地域別の売上予測を返す関数」のセルの中のコメントアウト部分に予測結果のテーブル名を入力し、コメントアウトを外す
    • 例) FROM workspace.default.forecast_predictions_ureshino
    • 「地域別の売上予測を返す関数」のセルの「▶」ボタンをクリック
    • 「指定された期間の月別・プロダクト別の売上のテーブルを返す関数」のセルの「▶」ボタンをクリック
    • 残りのセルの「▶」ボタンを順番にクリック

  3. 関数作成完了を確認 

⑦Playgroundでの関数設定

  1. 左サイドバーの「Playground」アイコンをクリック

  2. 必要に応じてLLMエンドポイントを変更(任意)

  3. Playground画面右側の「Tools」セクションを確認

  4. 「Add Tool」または「+」ボタンをクリック

  5. ツール選択画面で、UC Functionを選択し、手順8で作成した関数を選択 

  6. 「Add」ボタンをクリックして関数を追加

⑧初回分析の実行

  1. Playgroundのチャット入力欄に以下の質問を入力：
    「2023年4,5,6月の地域別・製品別の売上についてレポートを作成しなさい」

  2.  「Send」ボタンまたはEnterキーで送信

  3. AIが関数を使用してデータ分析を実行し、レポートを生成することを確認

     

⑨予測分析の実行

  1. AutoMLの実行が完了したことを確認（手順⑤）

  2. Playgroundで以下の質問を入力：
    「2024年4,5,6月の地域別・製品別の売上についてレポートを作成し、著しい増減があれば指摘しなさい。また2024年7月の売上予測を加味して、特に注意が必要な地域を指摘してください。」

  3. 「Send」ボタンで送信

  4. AIが売上データの分析と予測結果を組み合わせた包括的なレポートを生成することを確認 

      
    これでAIアシスタントが完成しました！

⑩AIアシスタントのAPI Endpointの作成

  1. 「コードを取得」を押し、「エージェントノートブックを作成する」を選択
  2. 必要に応じて 「Register the model to Unity Catalog」セルにある catalog, schema, model_nameを変更する
  3. 「すべてを実行」を押し、Notebookを実行する
  4.  「サービング」を押し、API Endpointが作成されたことを確認
  5. 作成したエンドポイントをクリックする。コピーボタンを押し、API Endpointをコピーする
  6. 外部のシステムからこのエンドポイントを利用する場合、必要に応じてプロフィールからトークンを作成する 

以上で手順は終了です。お疲れ様でした。

次回のブログでは、今回作成したAPI Endpointを利用して、Joule Skillを作成し、JouleからSAP Databricksの予測を呼び出します！お楽しみに🙂

最後までお読みいただきありがとうございました。

entry.title: Joule for Consultants - Success Metrics
entry.content:
While we all have started using J4C , it is quite important to know how the success is meaured & evaluted in terms of J4C's adoption.https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-assistant/sap-consulting-capability.html 

Measuring the success of J4C requires establishing comprehensive Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) that evaluate user adoption, business impact, and technical performance. The measurement approach should cover KPIs that monitor influencing factors on user adoption both before and during the go-live phase, as well as KPIs to assess actual user adoption post go-live.

While J4C is still evolving but it has become one of the most important resources /tool for functional consultants as it caters to all types of projects like Greenfield , Upgrades, Migration , Conversion and many more.

I have tried to articulate possible metrics criteria in a tabular format.

Metric Category

Target Value

Measurement Period

Success Indicator

Consultant Adoption

80%+ active users

8-10 weeks during the project engagement

High engagement levels , Peer learning

Business Impact

15%+ productivity /operational efficiency gain

All project phases-implementation

Measurable efficiency improvements

Measurable efficiency improvements

99%+ accurate responses

Ongoing

Reliable tool performance

User Satisfaction

4.0+ rating (5-point scale) as an example

Monthly surveys

Positive user experience

entry.title: RAG For Document Analysis
entry.content:
Introduction

Large Language Models (LLMs) have transformed how we interact with data, enabling natural language understanding and generation at unprecedented levels. Yet, despite their capabilities, they are inherently limited by the knowledge embedded during training and often referred to as parametric memory. This limitation becomes evident when models are tasked with answering questions that require up-to-date, proprietary, or domain-specific information.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) emerges as a powerful paradigm to address this gap. By integrating external knowledge sources into the generation process, RAG enables LLMs to produce responses that are not only coherent but also factually grounded and contextually relevant.

Understanding the RAG Paradigm

At its core, RAG separates knowledge storage from reasoning capability.

  • Parametric Knowledge: Encoded within the model during training
  • Non-Parametric Knowledge: Stored externally in a searchable repository

This separation allows systems to remain flexible and continuously updated without the need for expensive retraining.

A useful analogy is the distinction between a closed-book exam and an open-book exam. Traditional LLMs operate like the former, relying solely on what they have learned. RAG-enabled systems, however, can reference external materials, enabling more accurate and informed responses.

The RAG Workflow

 

 

A typical RAG pipeline consists of these stages: 

  • Document Ingestion and Chunking

The process begins with ingesting documents from various sources such as PDFs, Word files, or structured data formats. These documents are divided into smaller, manageable segments or chunks. Effective chunking is critical, as it directly impacts the quality of retrieval.

  • Embedding Generation

Each chunk is transformed into a vector representation using an embedding model. These embeddings capture the semantic meaning of the text, allowing similar pieces of information to be located efficiently.

  • Vector Storage

The embeddings are stored in a vector database, such as Chroma, FAISS, or Pinecone. This database functions as an external memory layer, enabling rapid similarity searches.

  • Retrieval

When a user submits a query, it is converted into an embedding and compared against the stored vectors. The system retrieves the top-k most relevant chunks, forming the contextual foundation for the response.

  • Augmentation

The retrieved content is combined with the user query using a structured prompt template. This step ensures the language model has access to the most relevant information before generating a response.

  • Generation

Finally, the augmented prompt is passed to the LLM, which synthesizes a response grounded in both its internal knowledge and the retrieved context.

Why RAG Over Fine-Tuning?

Historically, adapting models to domain-specific tasks required fine-tuning, a process that is resource-intensive and inflexible. RAG offers a more efficient alternative:

  • Dynamic Knowledge Updates: No need to retrain the model when data changes
  • Cost Efficiency: Eliminates repeated training cycles
  • Scalability: Easily adapts to growing datasets
  • Modularity: Components can be independently optimized

This makes RAG particularly well-suited for environments where information evolves rapidly.

Benefits of RAG

Organizations adopting RAG can expect several advantages:

  • Improved Accuracy: Responses are grounded in real, retrievable data
  • Reduced Hallucinations: Minimizes unsupported or fabricated outputs
  • Real-Time Relevance: Easily incorporates the latest information
  • Transparency: Enables traceability to source documents
  • Domain Adaptability: Works seamlessly with specialized datasets

Challenges and Considerations

Despite its advantages, implementing RAG effectively requires careful design:

  • Chunking Strategy: Poor segmentation can degrade retrieval quality
  • Embedding Selection: Model choice significantly impacts performance
  • Latency: Retrieval adds overhead to response time
  • Prompt Engineering: Poorly structured prompts can limit effectiveness
  • Evaluation Metrics: Measuring success requires more than traditional accuracy metrics

Addressing these challenges is essential for building robust, production-ready systems.

Conclusion

Retrieval-Augmented Generation represents a significant evolution in the design of intelligent systems. By decoupling knowledge from reasoning, it enables LLMs to operate with greater accuracy, flexibility, and relevance.

For document analysis, RAG is not just an enhancement, it is a foundational capability that transforms how organizations interact with information. As data continues to grow in volume and complexity, RAG will play a central role in enabling systems that are not only intelligent, but also reliable and context-aware.

entry.title: SAP Joule: Conversational insights for production and process orders
entry.content:
SAP Joule introduced conversational capabilities directly into the Fiori Launchpad, and users can now interact with a SAP S/4HANA system using natural language. As of SAP S/4HANA 2021, Private Cloud Edition, Joule also supports manufacturing orders, and users can now use it to gain insights about production and process orders. The production scheduler can now use Joule to get, for example, a list of production orders for a material, show details of a specific order or get details about items or operations of an order.

This blog will show details about the features available for production orders.

Pre-requisites

First of all, Production and Process Order specific capabilities should be available as of SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud 2021.

In order to use the Joule capabilities described in this blog, you need to have Joule active in the system. You can find a detailed Joule setup guide in the following blog:

  • Joule for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition - A Comprehensive Setup Guide

The implementation of SAP Notes 3694923 and 3703307 is required to allow some actions to be completed in the respective Fiori App.

Users with access to Joule will need roles SAP_BR_PRODN_SUPERVISOR_DISC and SAP_BR_PRODN_SUPERVISOR_PROC to be able to use the production and process orders use cases.

Some use cases require the user to have an Area of Responsibility defined for the production supervisor. You can find more details about how to define an Area of Responsibility in the following link:

  • My Area of Responsibility – Production Supervisor.

Use cases

Joule supports the following uses cases for Production Orders:

  • Show all production orders
  • Show production order details
  • Show production orders for a plant and/or production supervisor
  • Show a specific detail of a production order
  • Show all items of a production order
  • Show all operations of a production order
  • Show master output material data
  • Find material details for a production order
  • Show statuses of a production order
  • Check status of a production order
  • Show all late production orders
  • Show all missing components of a production order
  • Show all late operations of a production order
  • Show all production orders created by <username>
  • Show production orders which satisfy multiple filter criteria including status, type of issue and date filters.

Examples

Now that we went through the pre-requisites and the supported use-cases, let’s see some examples of the use-cases described above. If you have Joule Active and enabled for your user, you should be able to access it in the Fiori Launchpad, by clicking the diamond icon in the right upper-right corner, as highlighted in the Figure below:

Figure 1: Accessing Joule in the Fiori Launchpad

 

We can start with a basic prompt, asking Joule to show all Production Orders for material SG224. As we can see in Figure 2, system shows a list of production orders for our material, and shows the button Open for each order.

Figure 2:  List of production orders in Joule.


If we click button Open, we will branch into the app Manage Production Orders, where we will be able to take actions for those production orders, as shown in Figure 3.

Figure 3: Manage Production Orders app

 

Even inside the app, we can still use Joule, and we can ask for details about a specific Production Order, as we can see in Figure 4.

Figure 4: Details of a Production Order


We can keep asking questions about this order, like the details about the order items and operations, and Joule will understand the context, even if we do not mention the order number again. In Figure 5 we can see how Joule answers when we ask about the operations of the order previously displayed.

  Figure 5: List of Production Order operations

 

Finally, Joule is also capable of answering more complex questions, and show production orders filtering by a specific status and plant, for example, as we can see in Figure 6.

Figure 6: List of partially confirmed Production Orders.

Similar use cases are available for Process Orders, and a comprehensive list of the available use cases with example can be found in the links below:

  • Production Order
  • Process Order

 

More information:

  • List of release specific Joule capabilities: https://help.sap.com/docs/joule/capabilities-guide/release-specific-capabilities
  • Joule Integration SAP Help: https://help.sap.com/docs/joule/integrating-joule-with-sap/integration-with-sap-s-4hana-cloud-private-edition
  • AI Functionality in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition: SAP note 3558794

 

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA Regional Implementation Group

entry.title: From Tracking to Acting: Strategic Insights from the SAP SuccessFactors Learning 1H 2026 Release
entry.content:
As SAP SuccessFactors rolls out its 1H 2026 Learning release, one thing is clear: we are moving from a system that tracks what learners do to a system that acts on it.

There's a long list of features in the release notes — and not all of them deserve equal attention. While it's easy to get caught up in the "what's new," I want to dig deeper into the "why now" — what these changes signal about where SAP is taking the Learning module, and what L&D and HRIS leaders should be doing about it before the rest of the market catches on.

Having spent the last two decades inside SAP SuccessFactors Learning — implementing, advising, and watching the product mature alongside some of the most demanding enterprise customers in the UK and beyond — I read every release through one filter: does this make learning more strategic, or just more featureful? With 1H 2026, the answer is genuinely the former. In several places, more decisively than I'd expected

Let me walk through the changes that I believe actually matter.

1. Agentic AI finally lands in Learning

For the last two release cycles, the conversation around AI in SuccessFactors has been dominated by Joule as a copilot — write this, summarise that, find me a course. Useful, but assistive. The 1H 2026 release introduces something materially different: Joule as an agent that takes initiative on behalf of managers and learners.

The Compliance Risk Mitigation Agent

This is the headline feature for me, and I suspect for most compliance-driven organisations. (You'll see it called the Learning Compliance Management Agent in the official documentation; SAP's product team also refers to it as the Compliance Risk Mitigation Agent in customer briefings, which I think captures the intent more clearly.)

The agent shifts the operating model of training compliance from reactive tracking — "who's overdue, who's about to be?" — to proactive execution. It surfaces who's at risk and why, sends pre-emptive reminders four weeks ahead of deadlines, and packages pending approvals into a guided action queue for the manager. On the learner side, it offers calendar-blocking suggestions so training commitments stop being the thing that always gets pushed.

If you've worked in regulated industries — pharma, financial services, transport, public sector — you'll know the pain this is solving. Compliance dashboards have always told you the bad news after it happened. Manager nudges have always been a manual workflow built on top of LMS data extracts. This agent collapses all of that into a single conversational interface inside Joule.

 

A few things to think through before you turn it on:

  • It requires Joule If your organisation hasn't yet committed to Joule, this release is a strong forcing function to do so. Worth noting that SAP has signalled it is actively reviewing the commercialisation of premium AI features — keep both the Premium AI and Base AI feature lists bookmarked, and don't model your 2026 budget on today's pricing.
  • It changes the manager workflow. Be deliberate about how this is introduced. Managers who currently rely on spreadsheets or emailed reports will need to be brought along.
  • It's an agent, not a magic wand. The quality of the recommendations depends on the cleanliness of your underlying assignment, due-date and approval data. If your curricula configuration is messy, the agent will faithfully reflect that mess.

Intelligent Q&A (Restricted Availability)

The second piece of the agentic AI story is Intelligent Q&A — an experience that lets learners ask natural-language questions and get answers grounded in your actual course, video, and document content, with multimodal references back to the source.

This is conceptually a significant move. Think about how much of your learning content is consumed today: a learner needs the answer to one specific question — say, how to resolve a process error on a particular machine — and the answer is buried thirty minutes into a two-hour module. Most learners give up. Some search SharePoint. Some ask their manager. Almost none complete the course just to extract that one answer.

Intelligent Q&A inverts that. It treats learning content as a queryable knowledge base, with the LMS as the trusted source of truth. The implications are bigger than they look:

 

 

  • Course completion stops being the only measure of value. If a learner gets the answer they need from the content, the content has done its job — even if the course was never started.
  • Content quality becomes the new bottleneck. AI-grounded answers are only as good as the underlying material. Old, contradictory, or low-quality content will get exposed quickly.
  • It changes what "good" content design looks like. Authors will increasingly need to think about how their material reads to an AI as well as to a human.

It's in restricted availability for now, which is the right call from SAP — this is the kind of capability that benefits from controlled rollout and customer feedback. If you have the appetite to be an early adopter, get on the list now.

2. Skills intelligence finally becomes operational

I've written and spoken extensively about the Talent Intelligence Hub and the broader skills agenda. The 1H 2026 release doesn't introduce a headline new pillar in this space, but it does meaningfully tighten the connection between Learning and the skills foundation. That's exactly what the strategy needs at this stage.

AI-Assisted Ratings for Inferred Skills

Skill inference for learning items has been available for a while. What's been missing is proficiency — the rating that tells you whether a course teaches a skill at a foundational level or develops it to mastery. As the SAP product team put it in the release briefing: without ratings, inferring the skill alone isn't worth that much.

This release closes that gap. The AI now infers both the skill and the proficiency rating, aligned to the levels defined in your Attributes Library. For organisations serious about skills-based learning recommendations, talent marketplaces, and personalised growth paths, this is foundational. Recommendations that respect proficiency are dramatically more useful than recommendations that don't.

Review AI-Inferred Skills — with a human in the loop

In parallel, there's now a proper review workflow. Administrators and content owners can create skills inference requests, monitor progress, and review the inferred skills and ratings before they're associated with learning items. You can also manually add skills from the Attributes Library during review.

The framing SAP is using here — human in the loop — is exactly the right one. AI inference without human review either doesn't get adopted (because admins don't trust it) or gets adopted too aggressively (and ends up polluting the skills graph). A structured review interface, with visibility into inference status, is the right balance — and it aligns Learning's skills association workflow with how the rest of the suite is moving.

Assisted Skills Association with OCN Items — Now GA

The AI-assisted skills association for Open Content Network items is now generally available after its early adoption phase. If you're consuming content from LinkedIn Learning, OpenSesame, Coursera, Udemy, or any other OCN provider — and most enterprise customers I work with consume from at least two — this matters. Provider skill taxonomies vary wildly. This feature normalises them against your Attributes Library so that, finally, content from different sources speaks a common skills language across both Learning and the Talent Intelligence Hub.

Together, these three changes are not glamorous. But they make the skills layer actually useful at scale, which is the precondition for everything from personalised recommendations to internal mobility to credible workforce planning.

3. Person-Centric Experience: a foundational architectural shift

This one deserves your attention even if it's not relevant to you today, because it tells you where the architecture is heading.

The Person-Centric Experience (PCX) introduces a person-based model in Learning — meaning a learner with multiple employments in Employee Central is unified under a single, system-maintained person record. No more switching between user IDs to see your full history. No more administrative gymnastics to merge users when someone changes employment status. Managers see a unified view across direct and indirect reports regardless of which employment they're accessed through. Administrators manage assignments, completions, history, and employment data at the person level, finally ending the cycle of internal employee user merges.

For organisations with large flexible workforces, multi-entity structures, contractor populations, or complex internal mobility patterns, this is the change you've been asking for. The Customer Influence ticket carries 267 votes — one of the highest I've seen for a Learning improvement, and a clear signal that this addresses real, daily pain.

A few practical observations:

  • It's restricted availability for 1H 2026. Available by request, with SAP approval required before configuration. This is the right caution — the migration is non-trivial.
  • The migration is real work. It involves merging duplicate user records into a single primary user (via a new User Merge Assistant), running a set of prerequisite validation checks (via the Migration Tool Assistant), and configuring a new Person Connector that replaces the legacy SF User Connector post-migration. The SF User Connector and system jobs are paused during the migration window, which can take from a few hours to two days depending on the size of your user base. Plan it like a project, not a switch. Reach out to us if you need help with this. 
  • Employee Central with sealed Learning–Platform integration is a prerequisite. If you're not there yet, this is one more reason to get there.
  • Start evaluating in preview now. SAP's recommendation, and mine. The earlier you understand what your data needs to look like, the smoother your eventual migration.

For TalenTeam clients with multiple-employment complexity, this is the moment we've been waiting for. We've built workarounds for years — TalentBox includes an app specifically for this — and while those workarounds will continue to serve, the platform finally moving to a person-based model is the right long-term direction. If you're interested in being part of the next wave of customers SAP enables for PCX, this is the right release cycle to engage.

4. Compliance and governance get sharper

 

A cluster of smaller features quietly addresses some long-standing compliance and governance gaps. None is individually a headline; together, they meaningfully reduce the surface area of "things that can go wrong."

Program Prerequisites & Approvals enforcement finally closes the loophole where item-level approvals and prerequisites could be bypassed when the item was launched via a program. If you've ever had to explain to an auditor why a regulatory item was completed without its required approval — because it was hit through a program path — you'll appreciate this. Tenant-level configurable, separate toggles for approvals and prerequisites, and 71 votes on Customer Influence say it all.

Curriculum Requirement Pool Retraining brings clarity to what was previously a confusing learner experience. Learners now see what retraining is due and when, and can launch the previously completed course directly from the requirement pool — no more clicking into the course details page just to start a re-run. Small change; meaningful reduction in support tickets.

 

Setting to Restrict Observer Selection to Primary Manager does exactly what it says. For task-based assessments where compliance integrity depends on the right person observing, this prevents learners from selecting an observer who shouldn't be in that role. Optional, opt-in, sensible.

E-Signature PIN learner self-reset has been a long-standing customer ask — 45 votes, and one I've personally raised with SAP more than once. It's now resolved. Learners can trigger their own PIN reset via two-factor authentication through email, no admin in the loop. Unglamorous, but anyone running compliance-heavy training in regulated industries knows how much admin overhead this single change removes.

Item Connector retains retraining information for revised items addresses a long-standing customer ask (121 votes). And bulk update of duration fields for online items via Item Connector (125 votes across two ideas) closes another real operational gap. Both are parity fixes that bring the connector behaviour in line with the admin UI — which is exactly what good integration design looks like.

These aren't strategic capabilities. They're the kind of "the platform should always have done this" improvements that, taken together, make the system meaningfully more trustworthy.

5. UX maturity: small changes, big compounding effect

The release continues the multi-year project of modernising the learner and manager experience. A few highlights:

Snack View on the Learning home page lets learners click into a card and see course, program, class, or curricula details — including ID, rating, description, associated skills, progress and next steps — in a dialog without navigating away. This is the right pattern for a modern learning home page: progressive disclosure rather than a forced page navigation for every detail check. It's an opt-out feature (default on), which I think is the right default. One thing to flag: Snack View can include custom attributes you've configured for your business context, so it's worth doing a careful UAT pass to make sure what surfaces in the dialog is actually what you want learners to see.

 

 

Keyword search in View All pages sounds small until you have a large compliance-driven assignment list. It searches by item title, ID, related curriculum ID and curriculum title — and in the Requests for Me section, also by the name of the user the request belongs to. Combined with type filters and a clear reset, it dramatically improves how learners and managers find specific items in long lists. Available across all sections except Recommendations.

Manager Enhancements restore several legacy manager links (View Reports, View Curricula Status, External Learning Requests), bring the team picker into clearer view, fix a registration permission conflict, and let managers remove assignments directly from team view. These are unglamorous, but if you've migrated managers to the new experience and watched their adoption stall because they couldn't find the actions they used daily, you'll know why this matters.

 

 

Program & Curricula Details Page experience uplifts modernise three of the most-visited pages in the learner journey. Opt-in via LMS_ADMIN, default off — so plan a review and rollout rather than letting them appear unannounced.

 

Learning Hour Analytical card on the new SuccessFactors homepage gives employees a near-real-time view of total learning hours for the year, with drill-down to the detailed report. Simple, but it's the casual visibility that nudges behaviour over time.

6. Quiet wins for admins and integrators

A few features in this release won't change a learner's day, but they'll change a Learning administrator's week.

Manage Super Admin Access Tool unifies super admin and admin overseer lifecycle management for Learning and Platform from a single page, integrated with SAP Cloud Identity Services. If you've ever struggled to keep super admin permissions consistent across the suite, this is a meaningful governance improvement.

Data Anonymisation Support for External Learners in Instance Refresh Tool extends existing anonymisation capabilities to external learners — important for any organisation with significant external learner populations (extended enterprise, partner training, customer training) running non-production instances under GDPR or equivalent regimes.

User Sync Validation in Instance Refresh Tool for Integration Center Jobs removes the exclusive dependency on the Employee Export job during Instance Refresh post-processing. More flexibility, less data integrity risk.

Extended AI Locales gives administrators a self-service generative AI route to translate default English labels for eleven additional locales — including Bosnian, Albanian, Azerbaijani, Basque, Kazakh, Mongolian, Colombian Spanish, Bengali, Khmer, Lao, Maori, and Latin Uzbek. For global enterprises operating in markets outside SAP's standard language packs, this is genuinely useful.

 

What I'm watching beyond the release notes

A couple of things worth tracking that aren't fully captured in the slides.

The xAPI integration for Learning is live in a limited Early Adopter Care programme in a small number of data centres, and SAP has indicated that the next wave of supported customers and DCs will be communicated shortly. For any organisation serious about modern content interoperability, this is worth watching closely and engaging on directly.

There's also a clear signal that more agentic capabilities are on the roadmap beyond the Compliance Agent. Customers already engaged in design advisory will know what's coming; for everyone else, the early adopter programmes for these are the right place to invest your time if you want to shape how they develop.

What it all adds up to

Step back from the individual features and a clear picture emerges. SAP is making three bets in this release that I think are all the right bets.

The first bet is that AI in Learning becomes agentic, not just assistive. The Compliance Risk Mitigation Agent and Intelligent Q&A signal a future where the LMS is something you converse with and delegate to, not just navigate. This is a significant paradigm shift, and the organisations that experiment early will be much better placed when these capabilities become table stakes in 12–18 months.

The second bet is that the skills layer needs to be production-grade, not a science experiment. AI-inferred ratings, structured human-in-the-loop review workflows, and OCN skills standardisation are the unglamorous plumbing that makes skills-based learning actually work at enterprise scale. If your skills strategy has stalled at the "we have an Attributes Library but the data is patchy" stage, this release gives you the tools to push it forward.

The third bet is that the underlying user model needs to evolve to support the modern, complex workforce. The Person-Centric Experience is the most important architectural change in Learning in years, and I'd encourage every customer with multiple-employment complexity to start engaging with it now — not in two releases when it goes generally available.

For learning leaders, the action list from this release is clear: revisit your Joule strategy, plan a Compliance Agent pilot, review your skills data hygiene, get on the PCX evaluation list if it's relevant, and use the various UX and governance improvements as a forcing function to refresh your adoption playbook.

This isn't a release that demands a rip-and-replace. It's a release that rewards organisations who treat the SAP SuccessFactors Learning platform as a strategic asset and invest in keeping pace with it. As SAP continues to evolve the Learning module — and the broader Talent Intelligence Hub it sits within — the gap between organisations that adopt thoughtfully and those that don't will keep widening.

The future of corporate learning is more conversational, more personalised, more skills-aware, and more genuinely intelligent than it has ever been. SAP SuccessFactors Learning continues to be one of the few platforms credibly building toward that future at enterprise scale, and 1H 2026 is a strong step on that path.

The future belongs to those who treat each release not as a feature update, but as a strategic checkpoint.

If you'd like to discuss what this release means specifically for your organisation — whether that's a Compliance Agent pilot, a Person-Centric Experience evaluation, a skills data readiness review, or engagement with the xAPI early adopter programme — I'd be happy to chat. Drop me a message on LinkedIn or get in touch via talenteam.com.

entry.title: Which Business AI solutions are available within my SAP Cloud Subscription?
entry.content:
In the last couple of months, I have been talking to many SAP customers looking to implement AI initiatives in their companies to improve productivity gains and/or because their management is asking about AI in their current business processes.

At the same time, generative AI models have made it easy for a set of SAP business end users to start prompting in natural language and create supplemental ideas for their day-to-day work, adding speed and pressure of AI to their IT counterparts or simply ignoring IT and creating their own tool domains.

However, we all know that while the accessibility of AI tools to SAP business end users is proliferating for idea creation, creating ideas via prompts doesn’t address all the companies’ fundamental IT policies and security. In the scope of the user, they might see local solutions to help them improve, but depending on the IT policies, they don’t know if the SAP business end users are aware that some ideas are creating potential challenges in security, data harmonization, risks, and costs.

In SAP, we built our SAP Business AI based on three core principles: Relevant, Reliable, and Responsible.

Relevant: AI is embedded directly into SAP's leading enterprise applications and business processes, providing contextually appropriate capabilities where users work daily.

Reliable: AI is grounded in unique business data and business process context, ensuring accurate, trustworthy outputs based on the most important enterprise data with intact business context.

Responsible: AI is delivered with the highest levels of concern for security, privacy, compliance, and ethics, maintaining human oversight and agency while addressing bias and ensuring transparency.

I know these core principles might sound philosophical, but they were designed to help our customers implement SAP Business AI without adding risks to their business. So why not look at anything SAP delivered out of the box first, which was built with these core principles in mind to protect your current investments.

So in this blog, I would like to expose you to where to look at all available capabilities and understand if these capabilities are included in your current Cloud Subscription or not, and also understand how you can move forward with them. Now, I recognize there are many blogs on some of the content already available, which I also recommend you read, e.g. SAP Business AI Catalog and AI Estimator in SAP Discovery Center , Unlock Base AI Package for SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud – Enjoy Joule and Embedded AI Features for Free, etc. However, my focus here is exploring AI use cases, understanding entitlements, and deciding how to move quickly to explore.

To run this case, let’s say you are an SAP Business Suite customer running a couple of SAP Public Cloud Solutions productively, including: SAP Cloud ERP (also known as SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition), SAP Integrated Business Planning, Concur Expense, and Concur Travel. Now you want to explore what SAP provides for artificial intelligence included in your cloud subscription.

So first you need to go to the SAP Discovery Center: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/ai-catalog/

SAP Discovery Center.

You can log in to your account if you have one. In the filter section, you will look for the commercial Types : Premium and Base.

Premium: Premium AI includes advanced and specialized AI services powered by AI Units. It enables more complex and scalable use cases such as Joule Premium and Document Grounding, helping organizations extend AI into higher-value scenarios across departments and workflows. In general, if you have not subscribed to these capabilities before, you will be able to add them to your contract with an additional pricing item.

Base: Base AI provides the foundational AI capabilities included in all SAP Cloud applications. These everyday AI features support core business processes—for example, generating insights, recommendations, or automating simple tasks across SAP solutions. Base AI is included in standard cloud subscriptions and can be used without limits or additional cost. So, in other words, you have the rights to use these capabilities as part of your SAP Cloud Subscription. You might need to add a subscription (e.g., Joule Base); however, this subscription comes at no additional cost.

So let's look at all Base capabilities for the system landscape (we are filtering the Discovery Center based on the date of release of this blog, so be aware more capabilities might have been included).

For SAP Cloud ERP:

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by SAP Cloud ERP and Base commercial type.

For SAP Integrated Business Planning:

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by SAP Cloud ERP and Base commercial type.

For SAP Concur Travel and Concur Expense:

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by Concur Travel and Concur Expense and Base commercial type.

So, putting it all together for our customer example (SAP Cloud ERP, SAP IBP, and Concur Travel and Expense), you have almost 40 use cases where you can enable AI within your current system landscape, with entitlements already included in your cloud subscriptions.

SAP Discovery Center – Filtered by SAP Cloud ERP, SAP IBP, Concur Travel and Concur Expense and Base commercial type.

So as indicated, you now have plenty of cases that you can explore with your SAP Business end users that were developed with the core principles that I discussed before: Relevant, Reliable, and Responsible.

Now, when you start exploring the AI capabilities, we have included all resources for you to see what is included, benefits, business value, resources for activation, other related products, and roadmaps.

This is important to review in order to provide your SAP Business end users with a clear explanation of what this capability will bring, along with the fact that since you are running a public cloud solution, you are technically ready to consume the capability out of the box or by just executing an activation.

SAP Discovery Center – Details on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edion, Joule (Base)

Agentic AI

As all industries are moving more toward autonomous agent capabilities to help drive the business, you can also use the Discovery Center to review what SAP has in store. Before you start with development of your own agent-specific capabilities, as indicated above, SAP Agents are also built with the core principles: Relevant, Reliable, and Responsible. So, before you embark on a development cycle of agentic capabilities using other tools in your landscape, I advise you to see what SAP offers out of the box.

SAP Discovery Center – AI Agents for SAP Cloud ERP, SAP IBP, Concur Travel & Concur Expense (Base & Premium).

Keep in mind that SAP Business AI capabilities are expanding quickly, so I recommend you check the Discovery Center regularly.

If you are an SAP Cloud ERP customer, I also suggest you look at this blog as it also presents some principles on AI that are relevant: Foundational Elements for Harnessing the Power of Business AI in Your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Ed.

I encourage you to discuss these points with your SAP Customer Success Manager, SAP Account Executive, or SAP Partner. They may be able to provide you with additional recommendations, guidance, and help you discover new ideas on SAP Business AI capabilities that can help you bring more business outcomes.

Your insights and opinions are valuable, so please don't hesitate to contribute by commenting on this blog.

Follow us via @SAP and #S4HANA or follow @Marco_Valencia (SAP Business Suite Customer Success Management).

entry.title: SAP CX Product Inspiration Series 2026: Execute CX as your Growth Stratetegy
entry.content:
Customer experience has entered a new era. In 2026, engagement alone is no longer enough. Businesses win by executing with intelligence, speed, and context across the entire enterprise. With AI embedded directly into sales, service, commerce, and engagement workflows, SAP Customer Experience is transforming CX from a front-office function into a true growth engine of your company. In this new Product Inspiration Series, we explore how SAP CX brings executional AI into the flow of work by connecting customer signals to real outcomes, powered by the business data and processes that run your organization.
The special character of the SAP CX Product Inspiration Series remains unchanged. Each video:
  • spotlights concrete innovations across the SAP CX portfolio
  • is created by product and industry experts, for fellow experts
  • opens with a short Q&A to set the context—the why, what, and how
  • follows a zero‑slides approach, focusing entirely on the product itself
  • delivers in‑depth explorations and hands‑on demos
  • helps you better understand the Consumer Industries and SAP CX product strategy
  • is crafted with passion and dedication for you—our customers, partners, colleagues, and CX enthusiasts

Now, let’s take a closer look at the first Product Inspiration videos that have crossed the finish line…
