1. Gainsight Customer Success Management for SAP Sales Cloud Version 2

 

Join Emma Falldorf, Product Manager SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 at SAP, for an exciting overview of the latest integrations between Gainsight Customer Success Management and SAP Sales Cloud Version 2. In this insightful video, we dive deep into the key benefits and usage scenarios of this powerful integration, provide a comprehensive look at how Gainsight enhances the customer experience.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:39 - Why did SAP invest in bringing Gainsight into SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?
02:06 - Which concrete scenarios does Gainsight offer for SAP customers?
03:09 - How can customers adopt Gainsight in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?
04:16 - Start of the Demo
04:32 - Demo: Sales-to-Success hand-over in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2
06:54 - Demo: Customer Success Management in Gainsight
10:27 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

2. Account Planning in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 in Action

Join Crystal Zhang, Product Manager SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 at SAP, for an exciting overview of the latest Account Planning capabilities of SAP Sales Cloud Version 2.
In this SAP Product Inspiration Video, learn why intelligent account planning is essential for modern sales teams and how SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 brings structure, insight, and focus to key account management. Through a concise end-to-end demo, the video shows how sales teams can capture customer goals and insights, define strategies and sales plans, monitor pipeline health, collaborate through action plans, and generate AI-powered executive summaries—all within one integrated solution. Discover how Intelligent CX from SAP helps turn account insights into consistent, measurable sales success.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
00:56 - Why Account Planning in SAP Sales Cloud Version 2?
02:19 - What scenarios does Account Planning support?
03:37 - How can customers adopt Account Planning?
04:26 - Start of the Demo
04:42 - Demo: Account Plan - Set Customer Goals, Utilize Notes and Surveys
05:56 - Demo: Account Plan - Define Business Figures
06:18 - Demo: Account Plan - Define Key Stakeholders
06:49 - Demo: Account Plan - Conduct SWAT Analysis
07:07 - Demo: Account Plan - Define Sales Plans and Targets
08:00 - Demo: Account Plan - Monitor Pipeline Coverage
08:13 - Demo: Account Plan - Specify Action Plans
08:43 - Demo: Account Plan - Track Progress in a Gantt Chart
09:00 - Demo: Account Plan - Generate AI-based Executive Summary
09:43 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

3. AI Scouting in SAP Sports One

Join Michael Kesselmann, Product Manager SAP Sports One, and Achim Ittner, VP for Sports at SAP, for an exciting overview on embedded AI scouting capabilities in SAP Sports One. In this video, you will learn how clubs and federations can leverage Generative AI to streamline scouting workflows, accelerate decision-making, and create high‑quality player summaries with ease.
Discover how SAP Joule enhances the scouting process by consolidating extensive written reports, answering performance-related questions instantly, and generating structured management summaries that support faster and more informed squad planning decisions.

The key highlights shared in this video are:
00:00 - Introduction
02:54 - Start of the Demo
03:38 - Demo: Scouting Reports in SAP Sports One
04:09 - Demo: AI-Assisted Scouting with Joule in SAP Sports One
05:05 - Summary: Intelligent CX from SAP

 

If you have any further questions please let me know. You can find more videos here as well as on our YouTube channel.

Bookmark this site as further videos will be published soon. 🚀

entry.title: New Learning Journey: Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance
entry.content:
Why This Learning Journey Matters

Artificial intelligence is rapidly reshaping how organizations operate, from automating routine processes to supporting smarter decision-making. Finance professionals who understand how to apply AI in real business scenarios are becoming essential change agents.

This intermediate-level learning journey is built for learners who already understand core finance processes and are ready to take the next step: applying SAP Business AI capabilities directly within SAP Finance scenarios.

What You’ll Learn

This learning journey provides a guided, hands-on exploration of SAP’s latest AI innovations for finance, including:

  • SAP Joule in Finance
    See how SAP’s AI-powered digital assistant supports day-to-day finance tasks and insights.
  • SAP Joule Agents
    Learn how specialized AI agents can assist with more complex and cross-functional finance activities.
  • SAP Joule for Consultants
    Discover AI-driven tools that help consultants analyze, explain, and design finance solutions more effectively.
  • SAP Business Data Cloud in Finance
    Explore how intelligent, harmonized data enables advanced reporting, planning, and decision support.

Throughout the journey, learners follow practical examples and concrete finance use cases, bridging the gap between theory and real-world application.

Who Should Take This Learning Journey?

This learning journey is ideal for Finance consultants and solution architects, as well as Finance business experts interested in AI-driven innovation. Basic knowledge of SAP financial business processes is recommended to get the most value from the content. If you’re new to AI in finance, SAP recommends starting with a foundational journey such as Discovering SAP Business AI Capabilities for SAP Finance, before continuing with this more advanced, application-focused journey. 

Get Started Today

👉Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance

Upskill, experiment, and position yourself at the forefront of AI-powered finance transformation.

entry.title: Joule A2A: Handeling Multi-Turn Conversations
entry.content:
In my first post on the Agent-to-Agent Integration with Joule, I showed how to set up a minimal viable example. Without context handling, your agent forgets everything after each turn—making follow-up questions or clarifications impossible. This post covers how to enable multi-turn conversations between Joule and your custom agent using the A2A protocol's context management features.

Scenario

The scenario is straightforward: we have a custom-built agent integrated with Joule. Joule is an assistant used by multiple users and capable of handling multiple conversations at the same time. If our agent is a static one—like the currency converter shown in my previous post—it's enough to handle each delegated task as an individual conversation without history. But for this blog we want to think of a scenario where the agent might need additional input from the user in multi-turn conversations. For this purpose we look at the contextId and taskId that the A2A protocol introduces, to sync the conversation context between Joule and our agent.

Understanding A2A Context Management

To support multi-turn interactions, the A2A protocol introduces contextId and taskId, which together allow agents to preserve conversational continuity and manage individual units of work across multiple requests.

Think of it this way:

  • contextId = the conversation. All messages between a user and agent that belong together share the same contextId. It's your memory key.
  • taskId = a specific job within that conversation. One conversation can have multiple tasks, each with its own lifecycle.
Example Flow
│
├── "Convert 300 USD to ?"
│     └── Task 1 (taskId: T1)
│           → input_required
│
├── "What is EUR rate?"
│     └── Task 2 (taskId: T2)
│           → completed

The agent creates both IDs. On the first request, neither exists—the agent generates them and returns them in the response. The client (Joule) then sends them back on subsequent requests to continue the conversation.

This separation allows clients to either continue a specific task, create a new task within an existing conversation, or manage multiple related tasks under one shared context—making A2A interactions stateful, structured, and scalable.

For multi-turn agents, tasks progress through defined lifecycle states

(working → input_required → completed)

Who manages these transitions? The agent's LLM does. When the model generates a response, it evaluates whether the user's input is sufficient to complete the task. If not, it signals input_required to request more information.

Find more details in the A2A Protocol documentation: https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/specification/#34-multi-turn-interactions 

Note: This blog post is referring to version 0.3.0 of the A2A protocol. 


How Joule A2A Implements Context Management

Joule uses the agent-request action type to communicate with A2A agents. This action gives us control over what parameters are included in the request body.

By default, Joule only sends the current user message that triggered the agent request—not the entire chat history. This is an important detail: your agent won't automatically see previous conversation turns unless you explicitly manage context. While you could potentially append history using capability context, the standard approach is to let the agent maintain its own memory using the context ID.

In addition to the user message, we can include contextId and taskId in the request body. Since Joule acts as an A2A client, its primary responsibility is to propagate these IDs back to the agent on subsequent requests. This is achieved using capability context variables—session-scoped variables that persist across multiple interactions within a Joule conversation.

One convenient detail: you might expect to need conditional logic like "if contextId is null, omit the parameter." But Joule handles empty values gracefully, so no special handling is required on the first turn when the IDs don't exist yet.

Let's look at how this works in practice.

Joule Capability Configuration

The capability configuration implements the context flow described above. Here's the structure:

currency_agent_capability/
├── functions/
│   └── currency_agent_function.yaml
├── scenarios/
│   └── currency_agent_scenario.yaml
├── capability_context.yaml
└── capability.sapdas.yaml

An important point to understand: the agent supplies the IDs, not Joule. On the first request, Joule sends empty values. The agent generates new IDs and returns them in the response. Joule then captures these IDs and sends them back on subsequent requests.

Here's an example of the first request to the agent — note that contextId and taskId are absent:

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": "cc09e1b1-0a32-4160-9bcb-3336227b8c05",
  "method": "message/send",
  "params": {
    "message": {
      "role": "user",
      "parts": [{"text": "Convert 300 USD to", "kind": "text"}],
      "messageId": "cc09e1b1-0a32-4160-9bcb-3336227b8c05",
      "kind": "message"
    }
  }
}

The agent processes this request, generates new IDs, and returns a response with input-required status:

{
  "body": {
    "kind": "task",
    "contextId": "98fa8d22-0e99-4efe-a1a9-fff535e7db9d",
    "id": "f0d78896-0eab-472f-8a85-73050948c0a2",
    "history": [
      {
        "role": "user",
        "kind": "message",
        "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "What is the exchange rate for USD?"}],
        "messageId": "eb8b5afa-63cb-4cc5-9b24-38f85e782c95"
      },
      {
        "role": "agent",
        "kind": "message",
        "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "Looking up the exchange rates..."}]
      }
    ],
    "status": {
      "state": "input-required",
      "message": {
        "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "Which currency would you like to convert USD to?"}]
      }
    }
  }
}

Joule captures the contextId and taskId from this response. On the second request, both IDs are now included:

{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": "87cc08ae-8fc3-4d5c-a4ab-9dafd77d23a8",
  "method": "message/send",
  "params": {
    "message": {
      "role": "user",
      "parts": [{"text": "EUR", "kind": "text"}],
      "messageId": "87cc08ae-8fc3-4d5c-a4ab-9dafd77d23a8",
      "contextId": "98fa8d22-0e99-4efe-a1a9-fff535e7db9d",
      "taskId": "f0d78896-0eab-472f-8a85-73050948c0a2",
      "kind": "message"
    }
  }
}

In subsequent steps the ID's are passed along accordingly. Lets have a look how we can store them in Joule across messages:

Capability Context Definition

For that, we define variables in the capability context - to hold the context ID and task ID. These variables persist across multiple interactions within the same Joule session:

# capability_context.yaml
variables:
  - name: agent_context_id
  - name: agent_task_id


Scenario Configuration

The scenario defines which parameters flow in and which capability context variables are set with the result. In here we add the two variables:

# scenarios/currency_agent_scenario.yaml
description: The Currency Agent supports converting between different currencies.
target:
  name: currency_agent_function
  type: function
  parameters:
    - name: agent_context_id
      value: $capability_context.agent_context_id
    - name: agent_task_id
      value: $capability_context.agent_task_id

capability_context:
  - name: agent_context_id
    value: $target_result.agent_context_id
  - name: agent_task_id
    value: $target_result.agent_task_id


Function Definition

The function handles the actual A2A agent request and it now needs to send the ID's and ensure they are read from the response:

# functions/currency_agent_function.yaml
parameters:
  - name: agent_context_id
    optional: true
  - name: agent_task_id
    optional: true

action_groups:
  - actions:
      - type: agent-request
        system_alias: CURRENCY_AGENT
        agent_type: remote
        body:
          contextId: "<? agent_context_id ?>"
          taskId: "<? agent_task_id ?>"
        result_variable: "_agent_response"
      - type: message
        message:
          type: text
          content: "LOG: Context ID: <? _agent_response.body.contextId ?> | Task ID: <? _agent_response.body.id ?> | Status: <? _agent_response.body.status.state ?>"
  - condition: _agent_response.body != null && _agent_response.body.status != null && _agent_response.body.status.state == "input-required"
    actions:
    - type: message
      message:
        type: text
        content: <? _agent_response.body.status.message.parts[0].text ?>
        markdown: true
  - condition: _agent_response.body != null && _agent_response.body.artifacts != null && _agent_response.body.artifacts[0].parts != null
    actions:
    - type: message
      message:
        type: text
        content: <? _agent_response.body.artifacts[0].parts[0].text ?>
        markdown: true

result:
  agent_result: <? _agent_response ?>
  agent_context_id: "<? _agent_response != null && _agent_response.body != null && _agent_response.body.contextId != null ? _agent_response.body.contextId : '' ?>"
  agent_task_id: "<? _agent_response.body.status.state == 'completed' ? null : _agent_response.body.id ?>"

Key implementation details:

  1. We pass the variables into the agent-requests' body.
  2. Null-safe contextId extraction: The result uses a null-safe check to avoid errors.
  3. Task state handling: The result block sets agent_task_id to null when the status is completed, so the next request starts a fresh task. Otherwise, it preserves the task ID for continuation.
  4. Response routing: Two conditional action groups handle different response types—input-required shows the status message (the question to the user), while completed shows the artifact (the final result).

Note: This function implements extensive logic to manage taskId transitions. This level of handling is not required for all custom agents. If only contextId-based conversation tracking is needed, a simplified example is available on GitHub, while this version is retained here for explanatory purposes.

The Python Agent Implementation

Now let's look at how the agent code handles the context ID.

Agent Executor

The AgentExecutor is where context management happens. The A2A library handles most of the heavy lifting. Here's the core of the execute method:

# agent_executor.py
async def execute(self, context: RequestContext, event_queue: EventQueue) -> None:
    query = context.get_user_input()
    task = context.current_task

    # Create new task if none exists (first turn)
    if not task:
        task = new_task(context.message)
        await event_queue.enqueue_event(task)

    updater = TaskUpdater(event_queue, task.id, task.context_id)

    async for item in self.agent.stream(query, task.context_id):
        if item['require_user_input']:
            # Multi-turn: signal that we need more input
            await updater.update_status(
                TaskState.input_required,
                new_agent_text_message(item['content'], task.context_id, task.id),
                final=True,
            )
            break
        elif item['is_task_complete']:
            # Task complete: return the result as artifact
            await updater.add_artifact([Part(root=TextPart(text=item['content']))])
            await updater.complete()
            break

The key parts for context management:

  • new_task(context.message) — generates a new set of ID's if none exists, or reuses the one from the incoming request
  • task.context_id — the persistent ID that links all turns of the conversation
  • new_agent_text_message(content, task.context_id, task.id) — every response includes both IDs
  • TaskState.input_required — signals Joule that this is a multi-turn conversation

The Agent with Memory

The actual agent uses LangGraph's MemorySaver to persist conversation state. The key insight is using the A2A context_id as LangGraph's thread_id:

# agent.py
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent

memory = MemorySaver()

class CurrencyAgent:
    def __init__(self):
        self.graph = create_react_agent(
            self.model,
            tools=[get_exchange_rate],
            checkpointer=memory,  # Enables conversation persistence
            # ...
        )

    async def stream(self, query, context_id):
        inputs = {'messages': [('user', query)]}
        config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': context_id}}  # Links A2A context to LangGraph memory

        for item in self.graph.stream(inputs, config, stream_mode='values'):
            # Process streaming responses...
            yield self.process_message(item)

The critical connection is:

config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': context_id}}

By using the A2A context_id as LangGraph's thread_id, all messages in the same conversation share the same memory. This is how the agent "remembers" previous turns—LangGraph automatically loads and saves the conversation state based on this ID.

Note: In this example the task_id is not used on the Python side—I included it in the Joule capability to demonstrate the full pattern. For synchronous interactions where one task follows another sequentially, only the context_id is needed for memory. The task_id becomes relevant for async updates or when you need to reference a specific task later.

Result

With the above implementation, our Currency Agent can now ask for additional input and receive it within the same conversation context.

We see the consistent contextId and how the taskId is changing when a task is done.

With this setup, your agent can now ask clarifying questions, gather information incrementally, and maintain context across multiple exchanges—all while Joule handles the routing and user interface. If you missed the basic A2A integration setup, check out my first post on Agent-to-Agent Integration with Joule.

Note: What we have not yet seen is our agent being able to ask Joule the orchestrator or other named agents via this interface for additional input. While in pure pro-code we already are able to call other A2A capable agents, the Joule standard Agents are not yet consumable. Announced at TechEd 25 this will change soon.

Find the full code on Github. If ou have any questions write a comment!

entry.title: A2A Agents on SAP BTP: Wiring the Agent-to-Agent Protocol into CAP
entry.content:
A2A Agents on SAP BTP: Wiring the Agent-to-Agent Protocol into CAP

A technical guide to building Agent-to-Agent agents natively on SAP Business Technology Platform

Google's Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol is quickly becoming a standard for agent interoperability. For SAP developers, this raises a practical question: how do you build A2A agents that feel native to BTP — authenticated through XSUAA, persisting state to HANA, reaching services through destinations — without reinventing infrastructure that already exists?

The answer might be simpler than expected: mount A2A directly into a CAP application. Not as a sidecar. As a first-class part of your server, sharing its runtime, its database, its service bindings. This post explores what that architecture looks like using TypeScript and the @a2a-js/sdk.

This will hopefully become a short blog series, from this introduction to the fully working project template.

The Project Structure

A CAP-based A2A agent follows a familiar CAP layout, with the agent logic living alongside standard services:

cap-based-agent/
├── db/
│   └── schema.cds                  # HANA entities for tasks, telemetry
├── srv/
│   ├── a2a-service.cds             # Read-only CDS service for monitoring
│   ├── a2a-service.ts              # Service handler (if needed)
│   ├── server.ts                   # CAP bootstrap — mounts A2A routes
│   ├── lib/
│   │   └── base-executor.ts        # Reusable A2A task lifecycle engine
│   └── agent/
│       ├── index.ts                # Your agent subclass
│       ├── agent-definition.ts     # Declarative agent config
│       └── agent-card.ts           # A2A agent card (discovery metadata)

If you've built a CAP application before, this looks familiar — and that's the point. The agent is a CAP app. cds watch works. cds deploy -2 hana creates your HANA tables. cds up ships everything as one artifact.

One Server, Two Protocol Worlds

CAP generates an Express application at startup. The cds.on("bootstrap") hook gives you access to that Express instance before CAP finalizes its own routes — and that's where A2A plugs in:

// server.ts
cds.on("bootstrap", async (app: express.Application) => {
    const agentExecutor = new agentDefinition.executorClass(agentDefinition.config);
    const hanaTaskStore = new HanaTaskStore();
    const requestHandler = new DefaultRequestHandler(
        agentDefinition.agentCard, hanaTaskStore, agentExecutor
    );

    // A2A discovery endpoint
    app.use(`${agentDefinition.path}/.well-known/agent.json`,
        agentCardHandler({ agentCardProvider: requestHandler }));

    // A2A JSON-RPC transport (the one Joule speaks)
    app.use(`${agentDefinition.path}/a2a/jsonrpc`,
        express.json(), jwtMiddleware,
        jsonRpcHandler({ requestHandler }));

    console.log(`🚀 Agent "${agentDefinition.name}" is running`);
});

After this, your application speaks A2A's JSON-RPC on one path and CAP's OData/REST on the others. One deployment, one HANA binding, one XSUAA instance. The operational surface area doesn't grow.

Persistence: Just CDS

An A2A agent needs to store tasks, push notification configurations, and — if you care about observability — telemetry data. In CAP, that's a schema file:

-- db/schema.cds
entity Tasks : managed {
    key id        : String(36);
        contextId : String(36);
        state     : String(20);
        data      : LargeString;
}

entity AgentTaskTelemetry : managed {
    key taskId           : UUID;
        contextId        : String(100);
        finalState       : String(30);
        durationMs       : Integer;
        toolsCalled      : Integer default 0;
        tokensTotal      : Integer default 0;
        errorMessage     : String(500);
}

The managed aspect gives you createdAt, modifiedAt, createdBy for free. Expose the telemetry entity as a read-only CAP service and you get a monitoring API without writing a single handler:

-- srv/a2a-service.cds
service A2AServerService @(path: '/a2a-service') {
    @readonly entity Tasks as projection on db.Tasks excluding { data };
    @readonly entity AgentTaskTelemetry as projection on db.AgentTaskTelemetry;
}

Query your agent's performance, error rates, and token consumption through standard OData. No custom dashboards required to get started.

Extending A2A Standards with CAP: The HanaTaskStore

The A2A SDK defines a TaskStore interface — a contract for persisting task state. The default in-memory implementation works for prototyping, but production agents on BTP need HANA. Here's where CAP shines: implementing a HanaTaskStore becomes almost trivial because you're working with CDS, not around it.

The TaskStore interface expects methods like get, set, and delete. With CAP's query language, each of these maps to a one-liner:

async get(taskId: string): Promise {
    const result = await SELECT.one.from("a2a.taskstore.Tasks").where({ id: taskId });
    return result ? JSON.parse(result.data) : undefined;
}

async set(task: Task): Promise {
    await UPSERT.into("a2a.taskstore.Tasks").entries({
        id: task.id,
        contextId: task.contextId,
        state: task.status.state,
        data: JSON.stringify(task),
    });
}

No connection pools to configure. No ORM mappings to maintain. CDS handles the HANA connection lifecycle through the same binding your CAP services already use. The same pattern extends naturally: a HanaPushNotificationStore for managing notification configs, a HanaCheckpointer for LangGraph's conversation memory — each one a thin adapter between an SDK interface and a CDS entity. This is the kind of extensibility that emerges when your agent framework and your persistence layer share the same runtime.

The Base Executor: Lifecycle Without Ceremony

The heart of the architecture is a base executor class that handles the full A2A task lifecycle. It manages LLM streaming, publishes status updates through the A2A event bus, handles interrupts when the agent needs user input, and deals with cancellation and timeouts. Here's what it looks like, simplified to the essential flow:

// lib/base-executor.ts (simplified)
export abstract class BaseAgentExecutor implements AgentExecutor {

    async execute(requestContext: RequestContext, eventBus: ExecutionEventBus): Promise<void> {
        const { taskId, contextId, userMessage } = requestContext;

        // 1. Publish "working" status
        this.publishStatusUpdate(eventBus, taskId, contextId, "working", "Processing...", false);

        // 2. Build messages — system prompt on first turn only, LangGraph
        //    prepends full checkpoint history on follow-ups automatically
        const messages = await this.buildMessages(userMessage, contextId);

        // 3. Stream the agent's response
        const stream = await this.agent.stream(
            { messages },
            { configurable: { thread_id: contextId } }
        );

        let lastAIContent = "";
        for await (const chunk of stream) {
            // Agent is asking the user a question — pause and wait
            if (chunk.__interrupt__?.length) {
                const question = this.getInputRequiredMessage(taskId, chunk.__interrupt__[0].value);
                this.publishStatusUpdate(eventBus, taskId, contextId, "input-required", question, false);
                return; // Checkpoint preserved — conversation resumes on next message
            }

            // Capture latest AI output
            if (isAIMessage(chunk)) {
                lastAIContent = chunk.content;
                this.publishStatusUpdate(eventBus, taskId, contextId, "working",
                    this.getWorkingMessage(taskId, lastAIContent), false);
            }
        }

        // 4. Publish final result and persist telemetry
        this.publishArtifact(eventBus, taskId, contextId, lastAIContent);
        this.publishStatusUpdate(eventBus, taskId, contextId, "completed",
            this.getCompletedMessage(taskId, lastAIContent), true);
    }
}

The key states are clear: working → input-required → working → completed (or failed/canceled). The base class manages HANA-backed conversation checkpointing for multi-turn dialogues, abort controllers with configurable timeouts, concurrency guards preventing parallel writes to the same thread, and automatic telemetry persistence after every execution. All of this stays invisible to the subclass author.

Your Agent: Override What Matters

Building a concrete agent means extending the base and providing just the behavior-specific pieces:

// agent/index.ts
export default class GreetingsAgentExecutor extends BaseAgentExecutor {
    constructor(config?: Partial<AgentConfig>) {
        super({ loggerName: "GreetingsAgentExecutor", ...config });
    }

    protected getWorkingMessage(taskId: string, thoughts: string): string {
        return `Working on it. ${thoughts || ""}`;
    }

    protected getCompletedMessage(taskId: string, content: string): string {
        return content;
    }

    protected getInputRequiredMessage(_taskId: string, interruptValue: unknown): string {
        if ((interruptValue as any)?.type === "ask_user") {
            return (interruptValue as any).question;
        }
        return "I need more information to proceed.";
    }
}

And then, the definition ties everything together declaratively:

// agent/agent-definition.ts
export const agentDefinition: AgentDefinition = {
    name: "greetings-agent",
    path: "/greetings-agent",
    agentCard: exampleAgentCard,
    executorClass: GreetingsAgentExecutor,
    config: {
        systemPrompt: "You are a friendly assistant that greets users using ASCII Code Art",
        orchestration: new OrchestrationClient({
            promptTemplating: { model: { name: "gpt-4o" } },
        }, { streaming: true }, { resourceGroup: "default" }, {
            destinationName: "ai-core-wr-artificial-intelligence-sdk",
        }),
    },
};

No API keys in environment variables. The BTP Destination Service handles credentials for AI Core access. The same mechanism works for any LLM available through the Orchestration API.

Rather than managing separate LLM integrations and credentials, we use the Orchestration Client abstracts away model selection, prompt templating, and fallback policies. Your agent configuration simply declares which model to use, and the Orchestration API handles routing, token counting, cost tracking, and seamless model switching without code changes. This makes agents portable across SAP's AI landscape — from open-source models to enterprise-licensed capabilities — while keeping sensitive model endpoints and API keys secure within the BTP infrastructure.

Leveraging the sap-ai-sdk/orchestration SDK, Agents can even leverage pre-existing Orchestration configuration: Prompt Engineers prepare the scenario in SAP AI Launchpad, developers wire it up into Agents with a simple ID.

MCP Tool Discovery Through BTP Destinations

An agent that only talks to an LLM is limited. The real power comes when it can call external tools. The architecture supports connecting to MCP servers through BTP destinations — add them to your agent definition and tools are discovered at startup:

config: {
    mcpServers: [{
        name: "My Tool Server",
        destinationName: "destination-to-mcp-server",
        path: "/mcp",
    }]
}

Each tool call could use principal propagation enabled destinations, so authorization is enforced per request:

// We can customize how the StreamableHTTPClientTransport triggers its fetches by providing a customized fetch function:
const baseFetch = (transportOpts.fetch as typeof globalThis.fetch) ?? globalThis.fetch;
transportOpts.fetch = async (input: RequestInfo | URL, init?: RequestInit) => {
	const mergedHeaders = new Headers(init?.headers);
	try {
		const userJwt = getCurrentJwt();
		const dest = await getDestination({
			destinationName: serverConfig.destinationName,
			...(userJwt && { jwt: userJwt }),
		});

		if (dest?.authTokens?.length) {
			for (const token of dest.authTokens) {
				if (!token.error && token.http_header) {
					mergedHeaders.set(token.http_header.key, token.http_header.value);
				}
			}
		}
		// Additional headers needed by the destination?
		if (dest?.headers) {
			for (const [key, value] of Object.entries(dest.headers)) {
				mergedHeaders.set(key, value as string);
			}
		}
	} catch (err) {
		this.LOG.warn(
			`Failed to resolve destination "${serverConfig.destinationName}" for tool call:`,
			err instanceof Error ? err.message : err,
		);
	}

The inbound JWT can be safely stored in a node's AsyncLocalStorage: the a2a-sdk/js uses Express, so a simple middleware will suffice:

export function createJwtContextMiddleware(xsuaaService: XsuaaService | null) {
   return function jwtContextMiddleware(req: Request, _res: Response, next: NextFunction): void {
   const authHeader = req.headers.authorization;
   if (!authHeader?.startsWith("Bearer ")) {
	next();
	return;
   }
   const token = authHeader.substring(7);
   // Do something with the JWT
}

Wired up to the a2a jsonrpc endpoint:

// JSON-RPC endpoint with middleware chain
app.use(
  `${agentPath}/a2a/jsonrpc`,
  express.json(),
  jwtMiddleware, // Extract the JWT before the a2a's jsonRpc handler
  jsonRpcHandler({ requestHandler, userBuilder }),
);

A Note on Language Choice

You can build A2A agents in any language — the protocol is transport-level JSON-RPC, so Python, Go, Java, anything that speaks HTTP will work. Python in particular has a rich ecosystem for AI workloads and may be the natural choice for teams with existing ML pipelines.

But it's worth considering what comes after the LLM call. An agent on BTP needs to persist tasks somewhere. It needs to authenticate users. It needs to resolve destinations for external services, manage push notification tokens, store conversation checkpoints. In a Python-based setup, each of these is a separate integration to build and maintain: an SAP HANA client library, manual XSUAA token validation, a custom Destination Service client, connection pooling, credential lifecycle management.

With CAP, these are solved problems. cds.run(SELECT.from(...)) talks to HANA through an already-configured binding. JWT verification hooks into the CAP middleware stack. Destinations resolve through the SAP Cloud SDK. The managed aspect on your CDS entities handles audit fields you'd otherwise write by hand.

The question isn't whether Python or other languages can do all of this — they can. The question is whether you want to spend your time wiring infrastructure or building agent behavior. When the platform already provides a programming model that handles the plumbing, there's real value in staying within it.

See the SAP Architecture Center article for additional reference.

What's Next

This architecture opens up interesting possibilities. Imagine agents that can leverage CDS-defined actions as tools, or that use CAP's ecosystem integrations to react to business events autonomously. The base executor pattern means each new agent is a subclass and a config object — the protocol and infrastructure complexity stays solved once.

Next, we'll dive deeper in the initial setup of the major building blocks to wire up the a2a standards with HANA.

Co-Authored with Haiku 🙂

entry.title: The Rise of Agentic AI: Transforming Enterprise Intelligence in SAP Ecosystems
entry.content:
Introduction

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is fundamentally reshaping how modern enterprises operate, innovate, and compete. What began as simple automation has now evolved into intelligent systems capable of learning, adapting, and making decisions.

Today, we are entering a transformative new phase the era of Agentic AI where AI systems no longer just respond to inputs but actively plan, reason, act, and collaborate to achieve complex business goals.

I remember when AI was mostly about crunching numbers for forecasts. Now, with SAP innovation,  it's stepping into full-blown independence. Companies using SAP S/4HANA stand out because they can weave these agents right into their daily grind, turning rigid processes into something flexible and smart

For SAP-driven organizations, this shift represents not just a technological upgrade, but a paradigm change in how business processes are executed, optimized, and orchestrated.

From Task Automation to Agentic Systems

The evolution of AI reflects a steady progression toward greater autonomy and intelligence. Each phase has contributed to building the foundation for today’s agent-driven ecosystems.

1. Rule-Based Systems: The Foundation of Automation

Early AI systems were deterministic and logic-driven. These rule-based systems relied on predefined conditions and workflows to execute tasks.

Examples included:

  • Basic chatbots
  • Workflow automation engines
  • Decision trees in ERP systems

While effective for structured tasks, these systems lacked adaptability and could not handle ambiguity or dynamic environments common in enterprise operations.

2. Machine Learning & Neural Networks: Learning from Data

The 1990s marked a significant shift toward data-driven intelligence with the rise of Machine Learning (ML) and Neural Networks.

That was a shift. Systems started pulling lessons from piles of data. Think IBM Watson showing off its thinking chops, or AlexNet flipping image analysis upside down with deep layers. What that meant was better guesses over time - spotting demand trends, sniffing out risks, keeping quality in check. In SAP, it popped up in supply chain modules or finance dashboards, making predictions feel less like guesswork.

Key breakthroughs included systems like:

  • IBM Watson, which demonstrated cognitive computing capabilities
  • AlexNet, which revolutionized image recognition through deep learning

These innovations enabled systems to:

  • Learn patterns from large datasets
  • Improve predictions over time
  • Support decision-making in areas like demand forecasting, risk analysis, and quality management

In SAP environments, this phase enabled predictive analytics and embedded intelligence within modules such as supply chain planning and finance.

3. Deep Learning, Generative AI & NLP: Human-Like Intelligence

Recent advancements in Deep Learning, Natural Language Processing (NLP), and Generative AI have accelerated AI adoption across enterprises.

Deep learning took it further, especially with natural language and generative tools. Large models now chat like people, whip up reports, or handle queries in apps. For SAP users, that translates to copilots that guide you through tasks or interfaces where you just talk it out. Insights come automatically, no digging required. Pretty handy when you're buried in data.

Large Language Models (LLMs) now enable systems to:

  • Understand and generate human-like language
  • Automate documentation and reporting
  • Provide conversational interfaces for enterprise applications

This has significantly enhanced user experience in SAP ecosystems through:

  • Intelligent copilots
  • Conversational AI interfaces
  • Automated insights generation

The Frontier: Agentic AI

But here's the exciting bit , agentic AI pulls all that together into something proactive. These agents don't wait for orders. They chase goals, weigh options in changing situations, loop in other agents or folks, and tweak as they go. No more hostile into narrow lanes. Instead, they orchestrate across tools, data, everything.

All these advancements converge into the evolution: Agentic AI.

 

Agentic AI systems are characterized by their ability to:

  • Act autonomously toward defined goals
  • Reason and make decisions in dynamic environments
  • Collaborate with other agents and humans
  • Continuously learn and adapt

Unlike traditional AI, which operates within predefined boundaries, Agentic AI introduces goal-driven orchestration across systems, processes, and data.

Why Agentic AI Matters for SAP Enterprises

In the context of SAP S/4HANA and intelligent enterprise frameworks, Agentic AI unlocks new capabilities:

  • End-to-End Process Automation
    AI agents can manage complete workflows from demand sensing to order fulfillment without constant human intervention.
  • Intelligent Decision-Making
    Agents can evaluate multiple scenarios in real time, enabling proactive and optimized business decisions.
  • Cross-Functional Collaboration
    AI agents can interact across SAP modules (MM, SD, TM, Finance), breaking silos and improving process integration.
  • Adaptive Supply Chains
    Agentic systems can dynamically respond to disruptions, demand fluctuations, and logistics constraints. From what I've seen in SAP environments, this hits hard on a few fronts. End-to-end automation stands out. An agent might sense demand, route orders, and wrap fulfillment all on its own, cutting out the handoffs. Real issue tends to be human bottlenecks - this sidesteps them.

    Decision-making gets sharper too. Picture evaluating scenarios live, picking the best path without delay. Proactive moves like that optimize on the spot. And collaboration? Agents bridge modules - materials management, sales, transport, finance - smashing those old silos. Processes flow smoother because of it Supply chains adapt faster with this. Disruptions hit, demands swing, logistics snag - agents adjust dynamically. No more reactive scrambling. It's what actually happens in practice for businesses leaning on SAP. They build resilient setups that bend instead of break.

Conclusion

The journey from rule-based automation to Agentic AI represents a fundamental shift in enterprise intelligence. Organizations leveraging SAP platforms are uniquely positioned to harness this transformation by embedding AI agents into their core processes.

The way I see it, this evolution from basic rules to full agents marks a deep change. SAP outfits are primed for it, embedding these smarts into core ops. Thing is, as we push toward self-running systems, agentic AI drives the edge - efficiency ramps up, agility sharpens, and staying ahead gets easier in a fast digital world. Turns out, the ones who grab it first will pull away.

As businesses move toward autonomous, intelligent ecosystems, Agentic AI will become the cornerstone of innovation driving efficiency, agility, and competitive advantage in the digital era.

entry.title: Activating Joule for Developers (J4D) – BTP Trust Store Certificate Configuration in STRUST
entry.content:
This blog is a continuation of the series on activating Joule for Developers (J4D) with ABAP AI capabilities for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025. It focuses specifically on the BTP Trust Store Certificate installation step — uploading the required .pem certificates into the SSL Client created during the destination certificate setup.

Prerequisites

Before following the steps in this blog, ensure you have completed the destination certificate configuration described in the blog below. A new SSL Client (e.g., J4DFIN) must already be created with the destination certificate in place.

  • Destination certificate configured and SSL Client created — Generate Client Certificate for Joule for Developers: ABAP AI Capabilities
  • Access to transaction code STRUST (Trust Manager) in the SAP system
  • Access to transaction code SMICM (ICM Monitor) for the soft restart
  • All required .pem certificates downloaded from the BTP Trust Store repository on GitHub

Background

For Joule for Developers to communicate securely with SAP BTP services, the SAP system must trust the certificate authorities used by BTP. These root and intermediate CA certificates are maintained in the official SAP BTP Trust Store repository on GitHub:

https://github.com/sap-software/btp-trust-store/tree/main/required/pem

All certificates listed under the /required/pem path must be imported into the SSL Client in STRUST so that outbound TLS connections from the ABAP system to BTP endpoints are established successfully.

Step-by-Step: Installing BTP Trust Store Certificates

Step 1 — Download the Required Certificates

Navigate to the BTP Trust Store GitHub repository and download all .pem certificate files from the /required/pem folder. These include DigiCert, SAP, Entrust, and ISRG root certificates.

Step 2 — Open STRUST and Locate the SSL Client

Open transaction STRUST in the SAP system. In the navigation tree on the left, locate and double-click the SSL Client that was created in the prerequisite step (e.g., SSL client J4D forABAP Test Subaccount or J4DFIN). The panel on the right will display the Own Certificate section, confirming the destination certificate is in place.

Figure 1: Trust Manager (STRUST) – SSL Client with Own Certificate (destination certificate) already configured

Step 3 — Import Each Certificate

Scroll down to the Certificate area. Click the menu path Certificate > Import (or use the Import icon highlighted below) to open the file selection dialog.

Figure 2: Click the Import icon (highlighted in red) to open the Import Certificate dialog

In the file dialog, navigate to the folder where you saved the downloaded .pem files. Select the first certificate file and click Open.

Figure 3: File selection dialog showing the BTP Trust Store .pem certificates (DigiCert, Entrust, ISRG, SAP roots)

Step 4 — Add the Certificate to the Certificate List

After the file is loaded, the certificate details (Subject, Issuer, Serial Number, Algorithm, validity dates) will appear in the Certificate panel in the lower right area of the screen. Verify that the certificate information is correctly displayed, then click the Add to Certificate List button.

Figure 4: Certificate details for DigiCert Global Root CA — click Add to Certificate List (highlighted in red) to include it
Important: Make sure the certificate information is still visible in the Certificate area before clicking Add to Certificate List. If the panel is blank, re-import the certificate.

Step 5 — Save and Repeat for All Remaining Certificates

Click Save (or press Ctrl+S). Then repeat Steps 3 to 5 for each of the remaining .pem certificate files. Once all certificates have been added, the Certificate List section will display all imported CA entries with their validity dates.

Figure 5: Certificate List populated with all required BTP Trust Store certificates (DigiCert, SAP Cloud Root CA, ISRG Root X1, SAP Global Root CA)

Step 6 — Perform a Soft Restart via SMICM

After all certificates are loaded and saved, the ICM (Internet Communication Manager) must be restarted for the changes to take effect. Open transaction SMICM and perform a Soft Restart (Global):

1. Open transaction SMICM.
2. Navigate to the menu path: Administration > ICM > Exit Soft > Global.
3. Confirm the soft restart. The ICM will reload the SSL configuration, including the newly imported  certificates.

Next Steps

Once the certificates are loaded and the ICM has been restarted, continue with the J4D activation by following the instructions in the blog below:

SAP Joule for Developers: ABAP AI Capabilities for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition

SAP Note 3627794
In case the program required for activating the client certificate is missing in your system, implement SAP Note 3627794 to apply the necessary correction.

entry.title: What Happens to Your Data When You Enable SAP's AI Features?
entry.content:
What Happens to Your Data When You Enable SAP's AI Features?

Ten years ago, I sat in a meeting with a customer who refused to have his SAP Analytics Cloud data stored on AWS. Live connections only. His reasoning was the Patriot Act. US-based infrastructure could potentially be accessed by US authorities, and he wasn't willing to take that risk. Encryption didn't matter. SLAs didn't matter. The data wasn't leaving his control.

I remember thinking he was being careful, maybe a bit extreme, but thorough.

Fast forward to today. Same type of customer, same IT leadership profile. And they're enabling AI features across their SAP landscape that actively send business data to US-based LLMs for processing. Revenue figures, customer names, pricing details. Prompt by prompt.

I'm not saying these are the same thing. Cloud adoption happened, there are contractual safeguards now, and the world moved on. But I do find the contrast interesting. Somewhere along the way we started asking fewer questions. So I thought I'd ask a few.

What I expected

I expected a clean answer. Something like: your data stays in the tenant, Joule processes things locally, nothing leaves the region. That's roughly what most customers assume when I bring it up.

But that's not how it works. At least not exactly.

Joule, the Generative AI Hub, the orchestration service. They rely on third-party LLMs like GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini and various others. All are accessed through SAP AI Core. Your prompt and the business context around it travel to one of these models for inference. 

None of this is hidden. It's in the docs. It just doesn't come up much in enablement sessions.

Simplified, the flow looks like this:

That "IF configured" in the middle is what I want to talk about.

Data masking is a module, not a default

SAP offers data masking through the orchestration service, powered by SAP Data Privacy Integration (DPI).

You have two options:

  • Anonymization: irreversible, the original value is gone.
  • Pseudonymization: reversible, the LLM sees masked tokens and the response is unmasked before you see it.

There's also an allowlist if you want to keep certain terms ("SAP SE", "Harvard University") unmasked. Useful, but every entry is a deliberate hole in your privacy layer.

I confirmed all of this directly with SAP's AI Core Product Management after a conversation at the DSAG Technology Days. They were responsive and pointed me to the documentation. Credit where it's due.

Here's what I want to highlight though: masking is optional. The SDK docs say so plainly. It's a module you configure in the orchestration workflow. If it's not configured, it doesn't do anything.

const orchestrationClient = new OrchestrationClient({
  llm: { model_name: 'gpt-4o' },
  // masking goes HERE, if you add it
  masking: {
    masking_providers: [maskingProvider]
  }
});

In short: No masking block, no masking. SAP's own tutorials describe data masking, content filtering, and translation as "optional orchestration modules." Not defaults.

And this isn't just my read of the docs. Jana Subramanian (SAP's APJ Principal Cybersecurity Advisor) published a detailed security architecture overview of the two inference paths in SAP AI Core. When he describes the orchestration pipeline for LLM calls, he says the guardrails apply "where enabled." That's the same point, from SAP's own side.

The question worth asking in your landscape:

did someone enable them?

What masking catches, and what it doesn't

Say masking is configured. What does it actually catch?

The DPI service recognizes specific entities: person names, email addresses, phone numbers. Classic PII. You can define custom entities with regex, so employee IDs matching EMP-12345 can be masked too.

But think about what actually flows through an SAP system:

  • Next quarter's revenue forecast
  • A customer's contract value
  • Pricing from an ongoing negotiation
  • A strategic project codenamed "Titan"

None of that is PII. None of it triggers standard masking. But it's all commercially sensitive, and most CFOs wouldn't want it sitting on someone else's infrastructure, even transiently.

Unless you build custom regex patterns for every case, this data passes through. In practice, most implementations won't.

[Update pending: I've asked SAP AI Core Product Management directly whether commercially sensitive data beyond PII is covered. I'll update once I hear back.]

The "no training" promise

SAP confirmed that third-party LLM providers don't use your data for model training. This is handled through SAP's vendor onboarding process with standardized contracts.

The phrasing was that "opting out on the 'use data for training' is possible" through these agreements. Note the word "possible." It's careful wording.

This is a contractual guarantee, not a technical one. Your data still reaches the LLM provider's infrastructure and gets processed there. It's just not retained or used for training afterwards.

For most customers, that's fine. But my careful customer from ten years ago would probably ask: what happens if the contract changes? If the vendor gets acquired? If a subprocessor enters the chain?

Those aren't paranoid questions. They're standard DPO questions.

[Update pending: I've also asked whether this opt-out is the default for all SAP-served models, or whether each customer needs to request it.]

There are other options (and they might be enough)

Here's something I think gets lost in the Joule excitement: not every use case needs GPT-4o.

SAP AI Core distinguishes between models hosted on AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, or GCP Vertex AI (all US hyperscalers), and models that are "hosted and managed by SAP" directly. Mistral falls in the second group. So do IBM, Nvidia, and a set of open-source models.

For a lot of common tasks (summarization, classification, simple Q&A, document generation with templates) an open-source or EU-hosted model is often enough. Sometimes more than enough. And if you want to go further, you can run your own open-source model entirely on your own infrastructure or in a self-managed BTP deployment. No third-party LLM provider in the chain at all.

This is the part I'd push on if you're in a regulated industry or a Mittelstand company with strict data governance: the "use a US hyperscaler LLM" path is the default, but it's not the only path. The orchestration layer's harmonized API makes switching models a configuration change, not a rewrite.

Use what you have. Validate what you need.

[Update pending: And I've asked about inference location for EU-hosted customers. I'll add their answer here.]

What I'd actually do

If you're evaluating or already running AI features in your SAP landscape, here's my practical checklist:

  1. Ask your BTP team whether data masking is configured in the orchestration layer, or whether you're running on defaults. If they're not sure, you have your answer.
  2. Map your sensitive data beyond PII. Revenue numbers, customer names in business context, project codes, pricing. Then check if any of it is actually covered by your masking configuration.
  3. Understand the full data path. From SAP application, through the orchestration layer, to the LLM, and back. Know which model, where it's hosted, and what's contractual vs. architectural.
  4. Look at SAP-hosted or open-source models before defaulting to GPT-4o. A smaller EU-hosted model may fit your use case and your compliance requirements better.
  5. Check what your policy actually says. "No training" and "no transmission" aren't the same thing. Make sure you're meeting the real requirement.
  6. Make it an explicit decision. Whether you enable masking or skip it, document the choice. The worst outcome is a default that no one thought about.

Why now

Joule is being embedded across the SAP product suite. The Generative AI Hub is becoming a core platform capability. AI in SAP isn't experimental anymore.

That's why this conversation is worth having now instead of after everyone has enabled these features. SAP has built the building blocks for responsible AI usage. But building blocks need assembly, and right now the instructions are scattered across SDK docs, help portal pages, and conference hallway conversations.

My customer from ten years ago wouldn't host his SAC data on AWS. Today, that same level of care would probably serve a lot of organizations well. Not to block progress. Just to know what you're agreeing to when you click "enable."

Have you turned on Joule or the Generative AI Hub? What questions came up, or didn't? I'd genuinely like to hear how other teams are approaching this. 

Links:

  • SAP AI Core, Data Masking
  • SAP Data Privacy Integration
  • SAP AI Core, Security
  • Secure Data Flow for AI Inference on SAP BTP: RPT-1 vs LLM Orchestration
  • SAP Cloud SDK for AI, Orchestration
  • SAP Tutorial, Data Masking in Orchestration

entry.title: Beyond Joule Capabilities: A Knowledge Graph Driven Runtime for SAP Business AI
entry.content:
Beyond Joule Capabilities: A Knowledge Graph Driven Runtime for SAP Business AI

A working prototype and the idea behind it

IN THIS POST

  1. The scaling question
  2. The core idea
  3. The example
  4. Why this is different from Joule capabilities
  5. The architecture - KG as runtime brain
  6. Users do not think in system boundaries
  7. BDC - a potential path to faster coverage
  8. The honest complexity
  9. The prototype
  10. Closing thoughts

The scaling question

Today, building AI on SAP often means building capabilities one use case at a time. A new question introduces a new YAML definition, API binding, and deployment cycle. This model works well for many scenarios. But it raises a natural question: how does it scale as the variety and cross-system reach of user questions increases?

SAP's platform invitation is explicit: Joule as "an AI agent orchestrator," an agent builder for custom deployments, Business Data Cloud to "supercharge AI agents with the data that matters most, from any source." The infrastructure is there. This post explores one possible model for what to build on top of it - a working prototype running on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry, using a real A2A endpoint and a live PostgreSQL knowledge graph to answer retrieval questions without per-use-case authoring.

This is a personal exploration, not a product announcement. All views are my own. One constraint upfront: this covers read-oriented scenarios only - retrieval, explanation, analytics. Transactional write operations are out of scope.

The core idea

Joule owns the conversation. A2A owns the routing. The knowledge graph owns proven access patterns. OData owns the live data.

Each layer has one job. When a new system is added or a business term changes, only the relevant layer changes. The rest stays untouched.

This separation of concerns is the architectural backbone  and the reason the model handles question variability differently than a capability-per-use-case approach. The knowledge graph accumulates validated OData patterns over time; the A2A runtime draws on them at request time. Questions the KG already knows how to answer require no new authoring.

The example

A user says to Joule: "Show me my active purchase orders from supplier Bosch." No capability was pre-authored for that question.

Here is what happens:

 

Fig 1. The KG holds the pattern. The A2A layer matches the intent and composes the filter at runtime. Joule sees a card identical to any native capability.

The KG held the base pattern for active purchase orders. The slot extractor identified "Bosch" from the utterance and composed the OData filter at runtime.

The actual call:

The response template stored alongside the pattern in the KG rendered those records as a Joule card. The seam between Joule and the agent is invisible to the end user.

This leads to an important distinction from how Joule capabilities are typically built today.

Why this is different from Joule capabilities

SAP's Architecture Center documents two paths for extending Joule with custom agents: low-code via Joule Studio, and pro-code via the Bring Your Own Agent pattern -  where you expose an A2A-compliant HTTP endpoint and register it as a Joule Scenario. This prototype follows the pro-code path. What the knowledge graph adds is a runtime resolution layer on top of it: instead of one endpoint per use case, one endpoint resolves across all patterns the KG knows.

            TRADITIONAL JOULE CAPABILITY

             A2A + KNOWLEDGE GRAPH MODEL

  • One YAML file per use case, authored upfront
  • Hardcoded API, filter, and field selection
  • New question type = new capability version
  • Scale = more YAML
 
  • Patterns live in the KG, not in YAML files
  • Slot extraction composes filters at runtime
  • New supplier, year, or document: same pattern handles all
  • Scale = more usage
 

When to Use Each Approach

  • Traditional Joule Capabilities: Simple, well-defined use cases; single system queries; predictable user patterns
  • KG-Driven Runtime: Cross-system queries; high variation scenarios; exploratory business questions

The shift is from design-time authoring to runtime resolution. Governance changes accordingly: instead of "who owns this YAML?" the question becomes "who validated this OData pattern?" - answered by a domain lead approving a query-and-result pair, not a developer editing files.

Reference: SAP Architecture Center -  Integrating AI Agents with Joule documents the Bring Your Own Agent pro-code path, including A2A v0.3.0 protocol requirements and Joule Scenario registration.

The architecture - KG as runtime brain

Full architecture - A2A + Knowledge Graph on SAP BTP

Fig 2. End-to-end architecture: Joule routes via A2A to the self-hosted agent on BTP CF; the Knowledge Graph resolves intent and composes OData filters at runtime. Dashed boxes are planned / not yet GA.

 

 


Fig 3. The A2A layer is thin routing. The KG is where intelligence accumulates. The factory and runtime are the same flywheel - capability builds deposit patterns; the A2A runtime draws on them.

SAP's pro-code reference architecture for BTP agents also names a Knowledge Graph Engine - part of HANA Cloud - as the layer for encoding semantic business relationships. The PostgreSQL KG here is a developer-accessible analog of that concept: the same idea of accumulated, semantically enriched access patterns, running on a BTP-bound managed service rather than HANA Cloud.

Users do not think in system boundaries

"What is our total spend with supplier Bosch this year?" That question touches S/4HANA purchase orders, Ariba contracts, and Concur expense claims simultaneously. Users ask it as one question. Today it typically requires three separate lookups.

A2A offers a path toward answering it in one response - routing to specialized agents per system at runtime and composing the result. Each agent knows its own domain; none needs to know about the others. The same pattern applies across the SAP portfolio: procurement in S/4HANA, sourcing in Ariba, workforce in SuccessFactors, expense in Concur, external workforce in Fieldglass. The A2A routing layer is what makes cross-system answers possible without cross-system coupling.

One practical challenge emerges quickly: how to build initial KG coverage.

BDC - a potential path to faster coverage

The model works without BDC. But adoption will be slow. Building coverage through validated OData executions alone is a gradual process, and until the KG has enough patterns, too many intents fall back to "not yet supported."

BDC Data Products are designed to expose semantically enriched, consumption-ready models covering business definitions, domain taxonomy, and cross-entity relationships across SAP applications. If those models can be used to seed provisional KG entries, and live OData executions then promote them to validated, the cold-start problem becomes more manageable. Whether that seeding pipeline is straightforward to build depends on how BDC exposes its metadata in a given tenant, something worth exploring rather than assuming.

Raw OData tells you the shape of the data. BDC aims to tell you what the data means. That distinction could matter significantly when matching natural language to a query.

BDC also points toward a second channel - analytical questions that are difficult or impractical to answer through transactional OData alone, such as aggregated spend analysis or trend queries across periods. Those typically require pre-computed or aggregated data rather than a live API call. This is a natural conceptual extension of the same A2A routing layer, though not yet built in this prototype.

One important caveat: BDC's semantic model could provide a useful starting point, but tenant-specific customizations, restricted entity sets, and version differences would still require live validation before any pattern is trusted at runtime. BDC reduces the cold-start problem, it does not eliminate the validation step.

The honest complexity

Three areas are harder in practice than the architecture suggests.

Intent matching

PostgreSQL full-text search on intent keywords is fast but brittle on synonyms: "open orders" and "not invoiced" can mean the same thing; "cancelled" means different things in different modules. Production-grade matching would benefit from hybrid search combining full-text with embedding-based vector similarity so that semantic proximity compensates for lexical gaps.

Slot extraction

Resolving "Bosch" from a free-text utterance to a SupplierID sounds straightforward. In practice, users type partial names, aliases, or misspellings. Production-grade slot resolution needs alias handling, fuzzy matching, and potentially a lookup against master data, making it functionally a mini-MDM problem.

Authorization

The prototype authenticates via XSUAA JWT but calls OData using a service token, meaning it does not enforce row-level authorization per user. The right production path is principal propagation through BTP Destination Service - running the OData call under the user's own identity. This is on the roadmap but not yet implemented. The SAP Reference Architecture notes that the production Agent Gateway path uses IAS App2App tokens with named user context; the delta between that and a service token is the same gap this prototype still needs to close.

                                What works well

                                       Real limitations

  • Deterministic: every answer traces to a validated OData path - no hallucinated field names
  • Fast: KG lookup + OData call + render is sub-second
  • Auditable: every response traces to a specific pattern, entity, and filter
  • Composable: each system's agent is independently owned and evolvable

 

  • Cold start: BDC seeding may help; without some form of pre-seeding, early coverage is thin
  • Intent matching: full-text search alone misfires on synonyms and ambiguous phrasing
  • Slot resolution: aliases and fuzzy matching needed for production-grade accuracy
  • Authorization: row-level security requires principal propagation - not yet implemented
  • Schema drift: S/4HANA upgrades can silently break existing patterns
 

 

Governance model (in the prototype): Patterns are proposed automatically when a query succeeds. A domain lead reviews and promotes to validated=true before the pattern is trusted at runtime. Validation evidence - query, response, timestamp, user context - is logged against each pattern. Patterns not executed in N days are flagged for review, not deleted.

The prototype

Running on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry: Next.js application, SAP AppRouter for XSUAA authentication, PostgreSQL knowledge graph, BTP Destination Service for OData connectivity.

 

Context: SAP's official path for custom agents connecting to Joule is the Agent Gateway - documented in the SAP Architecture Center as exposing Joule Agents via A2A 0.3.0 with IAS App2App authentication. The Agent Gateway is not yet generally available. This prototype takes the developer path in the interim: a self-hosted A2A endpoint on BTP CF using XSUAA JWT. When the Agent Gateway reaches GA, the authentication model and registration process would change - the KG and OData runtime layer underneath would not.

Fig 4. * Service token today; principal propagation (user identity through to OData) is on the roadmap. When Agent Gateway reaches GA, the entry point shifts from self-hosted AppRouter to SAP-managed endpoint - the KG and OData layers remain unchanged.

Dual protocol handler

The endpoint accepts both A2A JSON-RPC 2.0 and Joule's native agent-request format. Both converge on the same resolver:

 

The resolver: KG lookup, slot extraction, OData, render

 

The knowledge graph schema

Joule's contextId field carries pagination state across conversation turns. When a user says "show more," the skip offset and pattern ID are encoded in the context ID,  so the next turn re-runs the same OData query from the correct offset without a new KG lookup.

Closing thoughts

The pieces fit together reasonably well. The A2A protocol is documented and implemented in Joule. The OData catalog is rich and well-governed. BTP handles authentication and connectivity. The engineering gaps - vector search, slot resolution, principal propagation are real but well-defined.

For teams building on Joule and A2A today, this suggests a possible shift in focus: from defining individual capabilities to curating reusable, validated access patterns. The two approaches are not mutually exclusive ; capability builds are what populate the KG in the first place. But the longer-term value may compound differently when patterns are shared rather than siloed per use case.

The idea to sit with

This is not a replacement for Joule capabilities. It is a complementary layer - one that may make them more scalable over time.

The real question is not can this work?
It is: who owns the knowledge graph?

One possible direction for SAP AI is not simply more capabilities, but fewer, more adaptive ones backed by a shared knowledge layer that agents draw on at runtime. SAP is delivering the ingredients: Joule as orchestrator, A2A as the routing standard, BDC as the semantic foundation.

Who else is exploring this direction?

This post reflects a personal exploration and learning exercise building on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. All views are my own and do not represent SAP's positions, roadmap, or product direction. Code snippets are from a personal prototype and are shared for illustrative purposes only.

Co-Authored with J4C and Sonnet 🙂

entry.title: SAP Ariba Supplier Management & Risk 2605 Release Highlights
entry.content:
Hello SAP Ariba Supplier Management & Risk (SM&R) Community!

If you're interested in learning about some of the new features coming with our May release, you're in the right place. 

Let's take a look at what's new within SM&R this quarter...

 

SAP Ariba Supplier Lifecycle and Performance

Additional Authorizations for AI Copilot Joule Scenarios in Supplier Management (SLP-2412)

This feature refines the requirements for user group membership to see prompts for specific Joule supplier management scenarios. This refinement ensures that prompts related to specific aspects of supplier management functionality are only visible  to users with relevant permissions, in customer landscapes that include the applicable solutions.

 

 

 

Ability to Restrict the Number of Commodity, Region, and Department Answers in External Questionnaires (SLP-2382)

This feature enables you to configure the maximum number of answers process initiators can add to Commodity, Region and Department questions in the external modular questionnaire.

Process initiators can only add the maximum number of answers you specify when creating a process, ensuring that processes meet your business requirements. It is applicable for external modular questionnaire. If the data is pre-populated from the registration stage or initial values specified from the template, the selected items need to be within the defined levels in the template configuration. If the pre-populated answers exceed the specified range, you must remove the values that are outside of the defined levels in the template configuration. 

 

 

SAP Ariba Supplier Risk

Additional Joule Capabilities in SAP Ariba Supplier Risk (SRISK-1860)

Your AI copilot Joule integrates with the Risk Exposure API - making it that much faster to find information via natural language prompting.

Take a look at these examples: 

 

Enhanced Virus scanning capability in Supplier Risk (SRISK-1329)

This feature automatically scans all files uploaded during different stages of an engagement request for viruses and malware, providing enhanced security protection throughout the supplier onboarding process. When documents are uploaded during any stage of creating an engagement request, each file is scanned in real-time before the next upload can begin. This sequential upload process ensures thorough security screening of documents.

Previously, you could upload multiple files consecutively without interruption. The system now requires a scan to complete before you can upload the next file. Please allow a brief moment between file uploads.

 

 

 

Enhancing Risk Exposure API by Adding Standard and Custom Category in Response (SRISK-1856)

 This feature enables the Risk Exposure API to retrieve responses for both standard and custom risk categories for suppliers you monitor in SAP Ariba Supplier Risk. The responses depend on the parameter setting for Enable custom risk categories (Application.SR.Risk.EnableCustomRiskCategories) on your site.

The current version (v3) of Risk Exposure API returns responses regardless of the parameter setting. 

When the Enable custom risk categories parameter is enabled, the API returns data for your custom risk categories.

When the parameter is disabled, the API automatically returns data for the default risk categories: Environmental & Social, Financial, Operational, and Regulatory & Legal.

(The previous version (v2) of the Risk Exposure API returned responses only when the Enable custom risk categories parameter was enabled.)

 

Once these features are officially released (May 15th) you can locate additional implementation and technical details, by simply searching for the feature ID here: What's New (via SAP Help Portal)

 

 

​Stay tuned for our additional upcoming blog posts and follow me to stay informed on upcoming feature releases across SAP Ariba.

​​Thank you for being a part of the SAP Ariba community and we look forward to continuing our journey of innovation together!

 

 

​​

 

Resources:

What’s New - where you can find implementation and Technical Details for any of these features (once released on May 15)

SAP Ariba Connect - Release Readiness (Customers except CIM)

Readiness Community page (For CIM customers)

SAP Community for Intelligent Spend Management page

SAP Help Portal pages - Procurement and Network
